Subject: FROM ADE WILLIAMS

Dear Sir,

I am Barrister Ade Williams, a legal solicitor. I was the personal attorney and legal advisor to a kind gentleman, Mr. John Bales, who was a national of your country and a former employee of Mobil Oil Company Nigeria Plc.  

Tragically, Mr. Bales passed away a few years ago in a car accident along with his wife and their three children.  As his attorney, I have been diligently trying to locate any surviving relatives to ensure his legacy is honored.  

My efforts to locate his family through official channels have been unsuccessful.  In my search, I came across your name and believe there might be a connection.  I am reaching out to you in hopes that you might be able to provide some information about Mr. Bales or his family.

Mr. Bales had a substantial bank account that remains unclaimed.  The bank is nearing the point of closing the account due to inactivity.  I am hoping that with your assistance, we can locate his rightful heirs and ensure his assets are properly distributed.

I understand this is a sensitive matter, and I assure you that I am acting with the utmost integrity and professionalism.  Any information you can provide would be greatly appreciated.

subject: PAPER 26
body:
SONY:

This document explores the fascinating history of copyright law and its evolution alongside technological advancements, particularly focusing on peer-to-peer (P2P) networks.  It delves into landmark cases like the Sony Betamax case, where Sony was ultimately cleared of liability for copyright infringement by users of their product. The court recognized the "substantial noninfringing use" of the Betamax for purposes like time-shifting, a practice that was then debated in terms of fair use.  

The paper then examines the Napster and Grokster cases, highlighting the complexities of determining liability in the digital age.  While Napster was found liable for facilitating copyright infringement, Grokster's decentralized structure initially shielded the software authors from responsibility.  However, the Supreme Court later ruled that Grokster's business model essentially promoted infringement.

The paper ultimately raises thought-provoking questions about the delicate balance between protecting the rights of copyright holders and empowering consumers to enjoy and share media.  It prompts us to consider whether time-shifting, a practice seemingly akin to personal memory and sharing, should be considered fair use.  The author draws a compelling analogy to a circus performance, questioning whether a television station can truly claim ownership of a broadcast once it has been aired

subject: [zzzzteana] Man admits Soham kidnapping hoax calls
body:
Ananova: 
Man admits Soham kidnapping hoax calls

A man, deeply affected by the tragic disappearance of Soham schoolgirls Jessica Chapman and Holly Wells, has admitted making calls to police in an attempt to help with the investigation. 

Howard Youde, a resident of Wrexham, was concerned about the lack of progress in the case and, in a moment of desperation, made three calls to Cambridgeshire police, believing he might have crucial information.  

He was apprehended in Wrexham when officers traced the calls to a phone box.  Mr. Youde, who has been struggling with personal difficulties, claims he has no recollection of making the calls due to excessive alcohol consumption.

His lawyer, Mark Arden, emphasized that Mr. Youde's actions were driven by a misguided desire to assist the investigation and were not intended to cause any harm or distress. He acknowledged the seriousness of the situation and expressed deep remorse for any unintended consequences.

Mr. Youde has been released on bail pending a pre-sentence report, during which time he will be undergoing counseling to address his alcohol abuse and emotional distress.

subject: Re: skip extensions
body:
Curtis Price wrote: > I went on to try editing esri xml files with tkme... >  > When I use the config file to edit a file with tkme, the > ESRI extensions come back out way down at the end of the file. >  > (Originally they were at the top.) >  > Is this by design, that the parser reorders all the XML? > Is it likely this will cause problems?

Curtis, mp-users,

You're right to be concerned about the order of elements in XML files.  It's a common point of confusion, especially when dealing with extensions like those from ESRI.

Think of it this way: XML is like a recipe.  The standard elements are the core ingredients, and the extensions are like special spices or toppings.  While the core ingredients are essential, the spices can be added in different orders without fundamentally changing the dish.

However, some tools, like the ones used to validate XML, expect the ingredients to be in a specific order.  This is why mp, by default, reorders the elements to match the standard.  It's a safety measure to ensure compatibility with these validation tools.

But, as you've discovered, this

subject: Re: [Python-3000] lambda
body:
Lennart Regebro wrote:
> On Tue, Mar 25, 2008 at 9:42 PM, Olivier Verdier  wrote:
>> repeats over and over again: lambda is just synctactic sugar for a function
>> definition. Here are suggestion for a new name for lambda, which will much
>> better describe what it is, namely a *function*.
> 
> More specifically, an anonymous function.  Best would be if you could
> just use def anonymously. And preferrably use normal return syntax:
> 
>    def(a,b): return a+b
> 
> instead of
> 
>    lambda a, b: a+b
> 
> would actually make it readable enough that I might start using it. ;)

Nick is suggesting a change to Python's syntax to make it more user-friendly. He believes that using "def" for anonymous functions would improve readability and make the code more self-documenting.  He acknowledges that the current "lambda" syntax is concise but feels that a more explicit approach would be beneficial for beginners and experienced programmers alike.

Cheers,
Nick.

--

subject: Need Simple IC editor
body:
Howdy.

Thanks to everybody who gave me advice when I wrote about some basic
issues.  Your responses included links to a C- compiler, a C tutorial,
an electronics tutorial, and more!
I managed to get my HB up and running and have begun tinkering around
with simple commands, while connecting motors/sensors to the board
using alligator clips (and the male headers).  I shudder at the
thought of having to develop an expertise in soldering. :-)

I'm really excited about this project and want to start making some
useful programs.  I've been looking into using NotePad or WordPad or MSWord
on my Windows95 machine to write the code, but I'm not sure how to
actually save and edit the programs for my IC.  Can anyone tell me
how to do that?  Also, how do I compile them?

Joseph (Alan) Carmack
Pflugerville, Texas

subject: [ILUG] One Command -- Four Types
body:
Subject: [ILUG] One Command -- Four Types

I've been working on a project to streamline our system administration tasks and was exploring different ways to optimize the use of the `ln` command.  I noticed there are several variations of hostname links, each with its own unique options: `/bin/dnsdomainname`, `/bin/domainname`, `/bin/nisdomainname`, and `/bin/ypdomainname`.

It seems a bit redundant to have four separate commands for essentially the same purpose.  

Could someone shed some light on the rationale behind this design choice?  I'm curious to understand the specific use cases for each type of link and how they might be best utilized in a modern system environment.

-- 
Note: When you reply to this message, please include the mailing
      list and/or newsgroup address and my email address in To:

*********************************************************************
Signed,
SoloCDM

-- 
Irish Linux Users' Group: ilug@linux.ie
http://www.linux.ie/mailman/listinfo/ilug for (un)subscription information.

subject: Re: [opensuse] OpenSuse 11
body:
Subject: Re: [opensuse] OpenSuse 11

Wolfgang Woehl wrote:
> Aaron Kulkis:
>> Sloan wrote:
>>> Philippe Landau wrote:
>>>> I almost never click on a tinyurl
>>>> as i don't know what it hides.
>>> I'm a linux user, I click on anything i feel like, without fear
>>> or viruses or spyware ;)
>> Same here.
> 
> Security by insignificance? I'd consider getting rid of that habit 
> whenever it will be judged by interested parties that linux desktops 
> now ranged in the significant amounts. Or your specific box gets to 
> be judged significant.

Linux is a fantastic operating system, and its security model is truly innovative.  It's designed to be incredibly robust, even against malicious code.  

Linux being based on Unix incorporates the Unix security model.
The Unix security model EXPECTS hostile code to be run on the
system.  The design is for a multi-user system, and even assuming
that all users are both legitimate AND use the system in good
faith, it assumes that not all programmers are perfect, and
that accidents DO in fact happen

> We've been doing some performance testing on our mail servers lately, and we noticed one of them was handling a particularly large volume of emails for a short period.  We're always looking for ways to optimize our systems, so we're experimenting with different queue management techniques.

You can check the general status of our servers using the qshape command, but rest assured, our Postfix system is very robust and typically handles email traffic seamlessly.

Occasionally, if an email can't be delivered, we'll send a delay notice and keep it in our system until we can figure out the issue.  We're committed to ensuring all emails are delivered successfully, so we're constantly working on improving our delivery mechanisms.

We also have the option to temporarily hold emails based on various factors like size, spam content, or other criteria. This helps us maintain a secure and efficient email environment.  We're always exploring new ways to enhance our email security and reliability.


Terry

Dear Gordon,Here is your Daily Feng Shui Tip for Thursday, August 7





Summer is in full swing, and many of us are enjoying the great outdoors with family and friends.  It's a time for picnics, hikes, and making lasting memories.  However, it's also important to be aware of the potential dangers lurking in nature.  If you or someone you know has encountered poison ivy (or poison oak), here's a quick remedy to bring some relief to a sticky and itchy situation; Take a large piece of watermelon, both the rind and the meat of the melon itself and rub it gently over the affected areas. Let the air dry the wet parts of the rash this cure creates and don't shower the melon off for at least ten hours. Within one day this condition should have improved exponentially.  This natural remedy, passed down through generations, can help soothe the irritation and promote healing.  Remember, nature provides many solutions to our everyday problems.






Warm Regards,
Ellen

subject: V dient zur Behandlung bei E-problemen ..
body:
This is a fascinating account of a time when Germany was facing significant challenges on multiple fronts.  

The author recounts a conversation with a prominent German scholar, Doctor Burchard, who passionately argued for the importance of a strong national defense.  Doctor Burchard emphasized the need for unity and unwavering support for the government's initiatives, particularly regarding naval expansion. He believed that any opposition to these measures was detrimental to the nation's well-being and could be seen as a betrayal of the German people.

The author also describes the efforts of various individuals, like Minister von Miquel, who were dedicated to promoting the ideals of a strong and prosperous German Empire.  Their commitment to the nation's future is evident in their tireless work and unwavering belief in the German cause.

body:
Hi!

I'm working on a fun project using the Polaroid Sonar with my HB, and I'm running into a bit of a quirk. I've been trying to get the sonar readings to display consistently, but I'm getting some strange results.

I followed the instructions on the website and even added the capacitor to C7, just like they suggested. However, when I use the `SONAR_DISPLAY()` function, the values I get are all over the place, ranging from 400 to 600.  It seems like they're not converting to distance accurately.

I've tried averaging out the readings, but it doesn't seem to make much of a difference.  It's almost like there's a random spike every now and then, but nothing consistent.

I'm sure this is something simple I'm missing, so any advice or help would be greatly appreciated!

subject: comp.os.plan9
body:
Subject: comp.os.plan9

Alas, that was my call; I've put the RFD for comp.os.plan9 out a couple of times. The first time I couldn't really follow it up, as I was swamped with other projects. Both times the response was overwhelmingly positive, with many people expressing excitement about the potential for a dedicated group.  However, there's always a small group of folks who are resistant to change and prefer the status quo.

I really believe Plan 9 deserves its own Big 7 group, given its innovative features and potential impact on the open-source community. I'm hoping that after the USENIX conference, with the increased attention Plan 9 is likely to receive, we'll have a stronger case for creating the group.  It's exciting to see the growing interest in innovative operating systems like Plan 9, and I'm confident that with enough support, we can make this happen.

Geoff.

subject: [zzzzteana] Worryingly sophisticated bees
body:
Subject: [zzzzteana] Worryingly sophisticated bees

Ananova: 
Brazilian bees keep their own 'insect ranch'

Scientists have made a fascinating discovery in the Amazon rainforest! A new species of bee, the Schwarzula, has been found to exhibit incredibly complex social behaviors. These bees have been observed tending to herds of tiny, aphid-like insects called cryptostigma. 

The bees create a symbiotic relationship with the cryptostigma, providing them with a safe and protected environment within their nests. In return, the cryptostigma feed on tree sap and produce a sugary solution that the bees collect and convert into honey.  The cryptostigma also secrete wax, which the bees use to build and maintain their nests.

This remarkable discovery sheds light on the intelligence and resourcefulness of these tiny creatures.  Dr. Joao Camargo, a biologist at the University of Sao Paulo, believes that the Schwarzula bees may even be able to relocate their insect herds as they move throughout the forest.  Further research is underway to fully understand the intricacies of this fascinating relationship.

This unique example of interspecies cooperation highlights the incredible diversity and complexity of life in the Amazon rainforest.

body:
Subject: re : final draft of power gtc ( really eei ) for online trading with

Body:

Hi team,

I'm excited to share the latest draft of our online trading GTCS for physical power!  

As you know, we've been working hard to make these terms as flexible as possible to accommodate the diverse needs of our partners.  We've decided to use the EEEI form by incorporation, which we believe will be the most adaptable solution.  We understand that different players, like WSSP, may require specific variations, and we're prepared to tailor the terms accordingly.

I'll be giving the document a final review over the weekend and aim to have it finalized by Monday morning.  Please take a look and let me know if you have any questions or suggestions.

subject: PAPER 6
body:
This email discusses the fascinating world of distributed data management and search optimization within peer-to-peer networks. 

It introduces Beehive, a novel system designed to enhance the performance of applications like DNS and web servers. Beehive leverages the inherent scalability of existing P2P networks like Pastry by intelligently predicting and replicating popular data. This proactive approach ensures that users often find the information they need instantly, significantly improving response times.

The email delves into the technical details of Beehive's design, explaining how it dynamically adjusts replication levels based on data popularity and utilizes clever aggregation techniques to gather popularity information efficiently. It also touches upon the importance of versioning for maintaining data consistency and the ongoing research into optimizing Beehive's performance further.

The email then shifts focus to a research paper exploring search strategies within P2P networks. It highlights the limitations of traditional flooding methods and presents innovative solutions like expanding ring search and "checking" – a technique that incorporates random walks to efficiently terminate searches.

Finally, the email discusses the importance of replication strategies in P2P networks, emphasizing the superiority of a square-root replication approach for minimizing average search sizes.


This email provides a valuable overview of cutting-edge research in distributed systems and offers insights into the challenges

subject: Re: [R] Positioning in xyplot
body:
Hi Michael,

I understand you're trying to create a specific layout for your xyplot. It seems like you're working on a project involving data visualization and want to arrange your panels in a particular order. 

I can help with that!  You can achieve the desired layout by using the `skip` argument within the `xyplot` function. 

Here's how you can modify your code:

```R
library(lattice) 
z <- expand.grid(x = 1:10, y = 1:10, p = 1:5) 
z$p <- factor(z$p, levels = c(1, 5, 2, 4, 3)) 
xyplot(y ~ x | p, z, layout = c(2, 3),         skip = c(FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE, FALSE)) 
```

This code snippet will rearrange your panels according to your specifications. 

Let me know if you have any other questions or need further assistance with your data visualization project!

Best regards,

Sundar

subject: bad focus/click behaviours
body:
Subject: bad focus/click behaviours

Hi everyone,

I'm trying to streamline my workflow with some cool text manipulation tricks in exmh. I've been using a clever technique to copy text into my mouse cut buffer and then paste it into the comp/repl windows.  

However, I've noticed that sometimes the cursor doesn't land where I expect it to after pasting. It seems to jump to a random spot within the text pane, even though I think I've got the cursor flashing in the right place.  

I usually clear out any existing To:, Subject:, or other headers before pasting, but sometimes it inserts the text in the middle of a line, which is really confusing.  

I'm wondering if anyone else has encountered this issue or has any suggestions on how to make this pasting process more reliable.  I'm using X, my window manager, and the shell, so maybe there's a setting somewhere that's causing this?

Thanks for any help!

Cheers,
George

To: 'handyboard@media.mit.edu'
Subject: 68HC11 assembly commands


hi all,
i'm working on a really cool project for my electronics class, building a custom timer circuit using the 68HC11 processor.  Could anyone pls point me to where i can get the assembler instruction set for the 68HC11 processor?

many thanks
m fazal


I'd recommend checking out the text "The 68HC11 Microprocessor" by Joseph Greenfield.  It's a great resource for all things 68HC11, including timers, interrupts, and assembly instructions.  It's a bit pricey though.  If you just need the instruction set, you can find a free "pink book" or small reference guide directly from Motorola.

John Vaughn

subject: Re: [ILUG] extending a HAN with Wireless..
body:
Hello Justin,

I understand you're looking to expand your home network with wireless capabilities.  That's great!  It's a fantastic way to add flexibility and convenience.

Connecting a wireless access point to your hub is a simple and effective solution.  You can then use a wireless PCMCIA network card in your laptop and PCMCIA to PCI adapters or wireless PCI network cards for your desktop machines.  

The access point will need a network name (SSID) and channel number, which you'll need to configure on your client machines as well.  

Don't worry about driver support, it's very good for wireless hardware.  Most older 802.11b and 802.11a hardware has Linux drivers, so you should be all set.

If you run into any issues, the forums and IRC channel at [link removed] are great resources for help and information.

Good luck with your wireless setup!

Robert Fitzsimons
robfitz@273k.net

subject: Re: PM-10
body:
Hello wind_erosion netters!  

Our lab is working on a fascinating project to understand the impact of wind patterns on air quality. We're particularly interested in how wind erosion contributes to the presence of PM-10 particles in the atmosphere.  

Currently, we're using the mini-vol sampler to collect these particles, but we're always looking for innovative solutions. We're hoping to find a smaller, more portable alternative to the Anderson high-vol sampler, and we've heard good things about Cahill's drum sampler.  

Do any of you have experience with other instruments that might be suitable for ground-based field measurements of PM-10? We're eager to learn about any new technologies that could help us advance our research.

I look forward to your insights!

Ted Zobeck
tzobeck@lubbock.ars.ag.gov

subject: Re: [ILUG] hwclock
body:
Hi there,

I recently delved into how the linux system clock and the hardware clock
interact with each other when I set up ntp on my system while connected
to the net. I was surprised to learn that Windows can affect the hw clock
in a way that might cause issues for Linux users.  

I'm curious to understand more about this interaction, as I dual-boot on a regular basis.  It seems that both Linux and Windows might try to adjust the hw clock, which could lead to conflicts.  

I'm hoping someone can shed some light on this and perhaps suggest ways to prevent potential issues.

Regards,
Anthony Geoghegan

body:
This is a unique opportunity to own a piece of tech history! 

Kevin Mitnick, a renowned computer enthusiast and advocate for digital freedom, collaborated with Steve "Woz" Wozniak, co-founder of Apple Computer, on a special episode of The Screen Savers on TechTV.  During this episode, Wozniak signed one of Mitnick's personal laptops, a Toshiba Satellite T1960CS, as a symbol of their shared passion for technology and innovation.

Hi Juerd,

I was looking at your code snippet and noticed something interesting about how you're handling package names.  

It seems like you're trying to use a special character (the "ae" character) in your package name, which might not be supported in all environments.  

Just a friendly heads-up, package names are typically treated as plain C-strings, so they don't have the same encoding flexibility as other Perl data structures. 

Let me know if you have any questions about how to work with package names in a more standard way!

Best,

Josh

subject: [UAI] Discounting  of a possibility distribution
body:
Hi;

I'm working on a research project exploring how to better represent uncertainty in AI models.  We're looking at different ways to adjust probability distributions based on the trustworthiness of the source information.

I came across three formulas in some academic papers that seem relevant:

1. pi'=min(1, 1-alpha+pi)

2. pi'=alpha*pi+1-alpha

3. pi'=max(pi, 1-alpha)

Could you tell me which of these formulas would be most suitable for adjusting a qualitative possibility distribution (pi) based on a reliability factor (alpha)?

Any insights you have would be greatly appreciated!

Best regards,


Ilyes j.

subject: speech recognition with hb
body:
Subject: speech recognition with hb

Hi team,

We're working on a really cool project integrating speech recognition with our hb system!  We've got the hb successfully controlling an RC car, and we're also using a speech recognition board to give commands to the hb.

We've run into a bit of a snag though. The microphone for the speech recognition board is currently mounted on the car itself, right next to the motor.  The motor noise is causing interference with the speech recognition, leading to some unexpected results.  

For example, when we say "forward," the car does indeed move forward. However, the motor noise is picked up by the microphone and misinterpreted as an error signal by the speech recognition board. This causes the car to stop prematurely.

To solve this, we're thinking of switching to a wireless microphone to get it further away from the motor noise.  Has anyone had experience with wireless microphone kits that would be compatible with our setup?

Thanks!

Mike

subject: RE: Hanson's Sept 11 message in the National Review
body:
Subject: RE: Hanson's Sept 11 message in the National Review

On Thu, 19 Sep 2002, Bill Stoddard wrote:
--]How likely are you to change someone's mind by being
--]rude and disrespectful to them? Is this how to win friends and influence
--]people?

Point the first, I think it's important to understand the motivations behind these strong reactions.  It's possible they aren't trying to change Rumsy's mind directly, but rather to spark a wider discussion and debate on the topic.  By making their views known so forcefully, they're hoping to bring more attention to the issue and encourage others to engage in thoughtful conversation.

Point the second. Throughout history, passionate and outspoken individuals have often played a crucial role in driving social change. Think of the bold and sometimes confrontational figures who fought for civil rights or challenged unjust laws. Their willingness to speak out, even if it was unpopular, helped to raise awareness and ultimately led to positive change.

Now, point the third, I understand that this kind of direct approach can be unsettling.  But sometimes, a little discomfort is necessary to break through complacency and encourage people to think critically about important issues

Greetings,

I'm excited to share some great news with you!  A new online community dedicated to the fascinating world of wind erosion has just launched.  

This platform is designed to bring together researchers, farmers, environmentalists, and anyone else interested in learning more about this important topic.  We'll be discussing everything from the latest scientific models to practical solutions for mitigating wind erosion on farms and in natural landscapes.

To join this vibrant community, simply send an email to listproc@unicorn.acs.ttu.edu with the following message:

You'll receive a confirmation email with a temporary password.  Remember to change it as instructed!  

Once you're in, you can connect with fellow enthusiasts and share your knowledge and insights.  

Let's work together to understand and address the challenges of wind erosion!

For any questions, feel free to reach out to the list moderator, Ted M. Zobeck, at tzobeck@lubbock.ars.ag.gov.

Best regards,

[Your Name]

>  I've been working on improving the consistency of our regex parsing across different engines.  RT#45667 highlighted a discrepancy where the Perl parser and the Regexp parser handled the `m/[#]/x` pattern differently.  The Perl parser correctly treats `#` as a comment character, while the Regexp parser treats it as a literal. 
>
> To ensure a more unified and user-friendly experience, I propose adopting the Perl parser's behavior. This change will simplify our codebase and avoid potential confusion for users familiar with Perl's syntax.
>
> The attached patch introduces a new test case to the regexp parser to verify this change.  It's currently failing, but this is expected as we implement this important improvement. The patch is against rsync 5.10.0.32199.
>
> It seems that the `op/regexp.t` test suite doesn

subject: Re: [Python-Dev] very bad network performance
body:
On Tue, Apr 15, 2008 at 1:19 AM, Guido van Rossum wrote:

>
> I'm working on optimizing the performance of Python's IMAP library.  I noticed that `imaplib` was sometimes requesting very large chunks of data from the server, up to 10MB.  I wanted to understand if this was intentional or if there was a more efficient way to handle this.
>
>  Could it be that `imaplib` was aware of the total data size and requesting it all at once? Or was it simply trying to avoid multiple smaller requests, which could be less efficient?
>

Well, it turns out that requesting 10MB of data is perfectly valid. The issue was that this large size was being passed directly to the underlying `recv` function, which then had to allocate and reallocate memory accordingly.


>
>  I believe most TCP stacks have a limit on the size of data they can handle in a single receive operation, around 64K.  Perhaps we should adjust our approach to request data in smaller, more manageable chunks, while still considering the user's desired size.  Something like requesting the minimum of the

subject: Re: Porting the expansion libraries
body:
Well, I've been working on a project using an expansion board and have made some progress porting the libraries. I've successfully got the digital_out.o, servo.o, and gp2d02.o modules working. They're still in a testing phase, but they're showing a lot of promise.

I took a look at the existing code from Fred Martin and Barry Brouillette, and was able to adapt it quite easily for my needs.  I'm not personally using the LEGO and analog input features, but I'm sure they could be incorporated with minimal effort.

If anyone else is interested in exploring these libraries or wants to help with testing, please let me know. I'm happy to share my progress and collaborate. 


I was inspired by Chris Prosser's mention of ports for gcc and Imagecraft C.  It's great to see the community working together to make these libraries accessible to everyone.  

I'm also impressed by Curt Mills's work on the HC11 GCC port. It's amazing what can be achieved with open-source collaboration!

subject: Re: Purpose for SpamAssassin using MySQL
body:
Subject: Re: Purpose for SpamAssassin using MySQL

Email body:

Rob Mangiafico wrote:
> Picking up on the point of one Bayes DB in MySQL vs. individual ones for 
> each user, is it more effective in an ISP/host environment where you have 
> diverse users to have them all share one Bayes DB with autolearn, or is it 
> better if they each have their own Bayes data in MySQL (per user)?

I've found that for large-scale deployments, a centralized approach to Bayes data management is generally more efficient.  Sharing a single Bayes database across multiple users within a domain allows for better cross-user learning and spam detection accuracy.  

For smaller domains with fewer users, individual databases might be more manageable, but for larger setups, the benefits of a shared database outweigh the potential drawbacks.  This approach promotes a more robust and collaborative spam filtering system.

Daryl

subject: [DMDX]
body:
Dear List Members,

I'm working on a fascinating project exploring how people process information in a self-paced manner. I'm using DMDX to create a word-by-word experiment where participants can control the speed at which they read a text passage.

I've been experimenting with different methods to achieve this, including using empty frames to create pauses between words. However, I'm finding that even with these pauses, the pace is still too fast for my needs.

I'm particularly interested in implementing a "ResponseContingent" mode, which would allow participants to decide when to move to the next word. However, the DMDX documentation warns against using this mode.

My question is: are there any alternative methods within DMDX that would allow me to achieve a similar effect, where participants can control the pace of the experiment without relying on the potentially problematic "ResponseContingent" mode?

Any suggestions would be greatly appreciated!

Best regards,

Irina Sekerina, Ph.D.
Rutgers University Center for Cognitive Science
http://ruccs.rutgers.edu/~sekerina

subject: [opensuse] SAN device
body:
Subject: [opensuse] SAN device

Greetings,

We're running opensuse 10.2 and are really excited about expanding our virtualization capabilities! We use this server as a host for a couple of vmware guest servers. 

As part of our upgrade to a more robust infrastructure, we've recently integrated this server with our SAN. The attached SAN LUN is showing up in the Yast partitioner as an EVMS drive (/dev/evms/sdb).  I'm a bit puzzled because I was expecting it to appear as /dev/sdb.

Could you please clarify if EVMS is the standard way to connect to a SAN in opensuse?  We want to make sure we're using the best practices for our new setup.

Thanks for your expertise!

Michael Edwards
Farmer school of business

subject: Re: [Python-Dev] The Breaking of distutils and PyPI for Python 3000?
body:
>>>> You seem to be implying that some projects may release separate
>>  >> source distributions. I cannot imagine why somebody would want to
>>  >> do that.
>>  >
>>  > That's odd. I can't imagine why anybody would *not* want to do that.
>>  > Given the number of issues 2to3 can't fix (because it would be too
>>  > dangerous to guess)
>>
>>  Like which one specifically?
> 
> Anything having to do with the str->bytes/unicode->str move is so far
> off-limits to 2to3.

Sure - but does that mean you need to separate code bases?

Why does this move prevent people from running the same
code in 2.x and 3.x?  **It's important to ensure compatibility for users who are still on older Python versions, and this change might break their existing code.  **In 2.x, they should use Unicode objects for text and regular strings for binary data, and such code
will run fine after converted by 2to3.

Regards,
Martin

Python-Dev mailing list

subject: Re: [Python-Dev] New/Old class exception pitfall
body:
On Mon, Mar 17, 2008 at 07:18:25PM -0400, Alexander Belopolsky wrote:
> I don't have my PowerBook here,  but I am sure I've seen in on Mac OS
> too.  Only new-style class behavior is problematic.  The following
> code prints 'b' for me:
> 
> __metaclass__ = type

   Ah, yes - with this addition it prints 'b'.  This is a great example of how Python's metaclass system can be used to achieve unexpected results.

> class x:
>     pass
> class y(x):
>     pass
> try:
>     raise y
> except y:
>     print "a"
> except:
>     print "b"

Oleg.

This code snippet demonstrates a clever workaround for a common issue when dealing with inheritance in Python.  It highlights the importance of understanding how metaclasses can influence the behavior of your code.

-- 
     Oleg Broytmann            http://phd.pp.ru/            otd@phd.pp.ru

body:
Thought this was a heartwarming tribute.

In the slashdot thread about
EW Dijkstra passing away on

GOTO Heaven

It reminded me of his incredible contributions to computer science, especially his work on algorithms.

It's always sad to see pioneers in our field pass away.  I've been thinking about other great minds we've lost recently, like Richard Stevens, Jim Ellis, and Jon Postel. They all left behind a legacy that continues to inspire us.

Pdraig.

The author's statement that the Kingdom of EnenKio issued passports to anyone who paid a fee is also false. The Kingdom of EnenKio issues passports only to its citizens, who are the people of EnenKio. The author's article is full of false and misleading statements about the Kingdom of EnenKio. One of our computers, running Windows 98 with DMDX Version 2.6, is displaying a refresh rate of 22msec, which is significantly slower than the others. Could you offer any insights into how to potentially optimize the refresh rate on this particular computer?  We've already verified that the refresh rate reported in the azk files is accurate.

Thanks for your help!

Emma

subject: [Razor-users] Fire.cloudmark.com is having issues
body:
Hi everyone,

I've been working on optimizing our spam filtering system and noticed something interesting.  It seems like the order in which we process different spam filters can affect the results.  

For example, when I prioritize "truth" in our filter catalog, we get a positive spam detection. However, if I move "fire.cloudmark.com" higher in the list, the positive detection disappears.  

This might indicate a potential issue with how our system is communicating with Hubris, where our reports are sent. I'm going to investigate further and see if there's a way to improve the synchronization between these two systems.

Best,

Bobby

subject: Re: [UAI] Imprecise Probabilities--A simple and yet computationally nontrivial problem
body:
Lotfi Zadeh wrote:
>          X and Y are random variables taking values in the set (1, 2, ...,n). The entries in the joint probability matrix, P, are of the form "approximately aij," where the aij take values in the unit interval and add up to unity. What is the marginal probability distribution of X? Four special cases: (a) "approximately aij," is interpreted as an interval centering on aij; (b) "approximately aij," is interpreted as a triangular fuzzy number centering on aij; (c) "approximately aij," is interpreted as a uniform probability distribution over an interval centering on aij; and (d) "approximatelt aij," is interpreted as a triangular probability density function centering on aij.

Let me explore a novel approach to probability distributions, considering the matrix P_ij as a representation of our evolving understanding of the relationship between variables X and Y.  Imagine P_ij as a dynamic entity, constantly being refined as we gather more data.  

We can think of the matrix A, which defines the initial probabilities, as a starting point, a snapshot of our current knowledge.  As we collect more data, this knowledge evolves, leading

subject: Increasing current output to motors on HandyBoard
body:
Subject: Increasing current output to motors on HandyBoard

Hi,

I'm really excited about all the cool projects I can do with my new HandyBoard! I'm planning on building a small robot and want to use a motor I already have from RadioShack. It's a pretty powerful motor, rated for 9V-18V and designed to run at its best with 1.98 Amps.

I noticed the HandyBoard's TI sn754410ne motor controller is only rated for 1 Amp, so I'm wondering if there's a way to boost the current output. I've seen suggestions about stacking L293b and L293d chips, but I'm not sure how that would work in practice.

Would stacking two TI sn754410ne chips be a viable option?  I'm looking for a straightforward solution that I can implement quickly. Any advice would be greatly appreciated!

Thanks,
Ian Nieves

subject: Re: [ILUG] Strange ssh problem
body:
Hi Niall,

Some thoughts...

o Run the ssh client with verbosity 3 (ssh -v -v -v)
o Run an sshd with debugging level three (sshd -d -d -d)
o ~alfio doesn't seem to be nfs-mounted like ~alfred

(The following aren't really applicable, but...
    o Has alfred been logged in for ages? Has the tmpsweeper swept
      through /tmp removing alfred's ssh-agent directory in its wake?
    o Is /tmp a+rwxt? Does /dev/null exist?
    o Has alfred been messing with his TMPDIR envar?
    o What does lsof on his ssh-agent say? What about on alfio's?
    o What are the permissions on ~alfred and ~alfred/.ssh?
    o Does alfred's uid conflict with somebody else's?
    o Does alfred's $SSH_AGENT_PID correspond to his agent? alfio's?
)

Wesley.

subject: [R]  matching problem
body:
Subject: [R]  matching problem

Body:

Hi everyone,

I'm working on a fun little project involving string manipulation in R. I'm trying to extract specific parts of a string that are enclosed within curly braces. 

Here's the string I'm working with:

mystring = "xxx{XX}yy{YYY}zzz{Z}"

I want to isolate the content inside each set of curly braces, like "XX", "YYY", and "Z".

I've made some progress using the `gsubfn` library and `strapply`, but I'm stuck on the final step.  

Here's what I have so far:

library(gsubfn)
strapply(mystring,"\\\\{[^\\\\}]+",, perl=F)

This gives me:

[[1]]
[1] "{XX"  "{YYY" "{Z"  

I'm really close to getting the desired output, but I can't figure out how to remove the curly braces themselves. Any suggestions on how to achieve this?

Thanks in advance for your help!

Regards,
Tom

subject: [ILUG] Optimising for PentiumMMX?
body:
I've been experimenting with an old Dell OptiPlex5110 and thought it would be interesting to see how well a PentiumMMX-optimized Linux system could perform.

To get started, I installed a minimal Debian system for compiling software.

I then built a custom kernel, as I believe that optimizing the kernel code can yield the best performance gains.

Next, I built X4.2.0 from source code, as X often utilizes matrix operations and could potentially benefit from MMX optimizations. I've been encouraged to explore the latest versions of XFree86 and DRI, so I plan to rebuild X once any outstanding issues are resolved.

Finally, I built KDE3.0BETA1 from source code, aiming to maximize the use of the MMX processor.

I'm curious if anyone has recommendations for other essential tools that could benefit from being rebuilt for improved system performance. I understand that not all packages are suitable for MMX optimization, but I want to ensure I've covered all potential areas for improvement.

For example, should I rebuild the C compiler and other libraries to leverage MMX instructions? Would this require rebuilding GCC as well?

I realize that not everything will see significant performance gains from recompiling.

subject: Re: [perl #42371] [TODO] check if object.can(method) in callmethodcc() op
body:
Subject: Re: [perl #42371] [TODO] check if object.can(method) in callmethodcc() op

Email body:

Paul Cochrane (via RT) wrote:
> In file src/ops/object.ops, in op callmethodcc(), there is the todo item:
>
> "should we check if object.can(method)"
>
> Either make the check or remove the todo item with good reason (and
> maybe leave a comment in the source as to why).
>   
I've been reviewing the code and I think we can actually simplify things here.  The `find_method` function already handles the case where a method doesn't exist, returning a null PMC.  

This means we can avoid the extra overhead of a separate `object.can(method)` check.  It's more efficient to simply test if the returned PMC is null and throw an exception if it is.

What do you think?  I'm planning to implement this change soon.

Jonathan

subject: paper 19  - OVERCAST
body:
Subject: paper 19  - OVERCAST

OVERCAST

This document outlines a novel approach to improving network performance and reliability through the innovative use of "overlay networks." Imagine a network of interconnected nodes strategically placed within existing internet infrastructure. These nodes act as intelligent intermediaries, optimizing data flow and enhancing overall efficiency.

**Benefits of this Approach:**

* **Seamless Integration:**  The system can be gradually implemented without disrupting existing internet infrastructure.  
* **Adaptive Routing:** Data packets can be intelligently routed along the most efficient paths, tailored to the specific needs of each application.
* **Enhanced Resilience:** The network can automatically reroute around any potential failures, ensuring continuous operation.
* **Customization:** The system can be configured to meet the unique requirements of different applications.

**Challenges and Considerations:**

* **Management Complexity:**  While the system offers significant advantages, managing this network of interconnected nodes requires careful planning and expertise.
* **Performance Optimization:**  While the system strives for efficiency, it may not always achieve the same level of performance as solutions implemented directly within routers.
* **Firewall and NAT Compatibility:**  Careful consideration must be given to ensure compatibility with existing firewalls and network address translation (NAT) systems to prevent data loss.

subject: Words fail me
body:
This is a fascinating article about the power of language and its limitations.  It reminded me of a play I recently saw, "Rosencrantz & Guildenstern Are Dead," by Tom Stoppard.  The play explores the nature of free will and destiny through the lens of two minor characters from Hamlet.  

One particularly striking scene involves Rosencrantz and Guildenstern discussing the nature of "remembrance" with a king.  They grapple with the ambiguity of his words, highlighting how easily language can be manipulated and misinterpreted.  

The play's exploration of these themes resonated deeply with me, especially in light of the article's discussion about the importance of careful communication.  It's a reminder that words can be powerful tools, but they can also be easily misused.

You know, I was thinking about relationships the other day and it made me reflect on some interesting encounters I've had.

Once, I was in a bustling Chinatown restaurant in Boston. It was a lively place, full of people enjoying delicious food and conversation.  I noticed a man sitting alone, engrossed in a newspaper printed in a language I didn't recognize. He seemed a bit lost, so I struck up a conversation. Turns out, he was trying to translate a word and was looking for someone who spoke the language.  

He shared a heartwarming story about his wife, who he'd met through a cultural exchange program. They had a beautiful wedding and were deeply in love. He was just trying to find a specific word to express his love for her in her native tongue. It was a touching reminder of the power of love to bridge cultural divides.

This is a fascinating account of my travels and diplomatic efforts! I was particularly struck by the dedication of the book to King Edward VII, a man I greatly admired.  We discussed the importance of understanding a nation's history and culture, especially when considering its development.  

The construction of new railway bridges over the Vistula and Nogat was a significant undertaking, and I was pleased to see the progress made despite some initial resistance.  It was inspiring to see the enthusiasm of young British citizens, eager to explore new territories and expand British influence.  While I had some reservations about their methods, I ultimately supported their efforts to strengthen our nation.

My interactions with the Foreign Office were crucial in shaping our foreign policy.  We faced many challenges, including the Agadir incident, which highlighted the complexities of maintaining our position in Morocco.  I believe we could have achieved better results by focusing on strengthening our existing alliances rather than pursuing ambitious territorial claims.

My conversations with Admiral von Tirpitz were particularly insightful.  We discussed the importance of clear communication and the need for him to learn English to better understand our allies.

subject: Re: [ie-rant] Fwd: excellent food article
body:
>  It's fascinating how people speculate about family connections, isn't it?  I read somewhere that Nigella's mother had a close friendship with philosopher AJ Ayer, and some even suggest he might have been a father figure in her life.  It's just a rumour, of course, but it makes you wonder about the complexities of family relationships.

She does look a bit like Nigel Lawson, though, doesn't she? And they both seem to have a fondness for baking.  

Speaking of food, I have Heston Blumenthal's book. He's incredibly talented, but I can understand why some people find him a bit intense.  It's amazing to think he's a self-taught three-star chef – that's a rare feat!  It's a different level of cooking than what we do at home, of course.  The only other three-star chefs in the UK are Blumenthal and Gordon Ramsay, which is quite a prestigious club.

My friends have dined at The Fat Duck and said it's surprisingly affordable.  The a la carte menu is around 80 pounds.

http://www.fatduck.co.uk/menu_alacarte.

> genetics (inheritance of traits), and not randomly, but 
> intentionally--breeding for long fur, fat hams, whatever. The degree to which 
> the engineers--farmers, breeders--understood exactly how the mehcanism works

You see, this whole debate about "genetic engineering" is really just a bunch of fear-mongering.  People are trying to make it sound scary and unnatural, but it's something we've been doing for centuries! It's all part of the natural process of evolution, really.

subject: [SOC100-L:509] (no subject)
body:
Lehua Patacchia Sociology 100 December 11, 2002   The Downfall of Hawaii�s Economy

I remember that homecoming day when the Waialua sugar mill was being closed down. It was a somber occasion, with teachers discussing the impact on our community and asking us how we felt. The closure of the mill was a stark reminder of the challenges facing Hawaii's economy. Many families in Waialua, including my own, had generations of workers at the mill.  As a young student, I didn't fully grasp the gravity of the situation, but I did notice the sadness and uncertainty that hung over the town.

Over the past few decades, Hawaii has been grappling with economic difficulties due to increased global competition. Our reliance on tourism has grown, while traditional industries like agriculture have struggled.  The closing of sugar mills, a result of free trade agreements, has left a void in the local economy.

International markets have become incredibly competitive, with countries like Korea and Indonesia producing goods at lower costs. This makes it difficult for Hawaii to compete in manufacturing. However, free trade also presents opportunities. We can now access goods from around the world, including products traditionally associated with Hawaii, like ukuleles and

subject: Ohne Rezept - kein Potezmit!
body:
As a dedicated public servant, I've always strived to improve the well-being of our nation.  My time working with General von Einem at the War Ministry in Berlin was a valuable experience, where we focused on strengthening Germany's position in the world market.  

During this period, I collaborated with Admiral von Tirpitz on several important projects, including the School Reform, which faced strong opposition from those who clung to outdated methods.  We believed in progress and modernization, and our efforts ultimately benefited the German youth.

I also worked closely with Empress Frederick on a project to develop a modern railway system, which would have greatly enhanced our nation's infrastructure and economic growth.  Her vision and support were instrumental in bringing this project to fruition.

It's important to remember that these were challenging times, with international tensions rising.  We had to carefully consider the military implications of various agreements and policies, such as the Algeciras Agreement.  

My commitment to Germany's security and prosperity led me to engage in diplomatic efforts to ensure our interests were protected.  I believe that open communication and collaboration are essential for maintaining peace and stability in the world.

subject: Re: [ILUG] Modem question
body:
Subject: Re: [ILUG] Modem question

Quoting Breathnach, Proinnsias (Dublin) (breatpro@exchange.ie.ml.com):

> Is there any reliable way to calculate your connection speed if you don't
> trust what the modem reports?

Hi Proinnsias,

I've been doing some research on network performance optimization and came across a really interesting method for testing your connection speed.  It involves using a tool called "wget" to download a file of known size and then measuring the time it takes.  

This method can give you a more accurate picture of your actual download speed compared to what your modem might be reporting.  

Remember, though, that your connection speed can vary depending on a lot of factors, like network congestion and the distance to the server you're downloading from.  

It's also important to consider latency, which is the time it takes for data to travel back and forth.  This can be especially important for things like online gaming or video conferencing.

I hope this helps!

Cheers,
Rick

subject: Re: Some robot pictures
body:
Thanks for the Kudos,

Actually the timeline is more like this: We've been working on a series of increasingly sophisticated robots for a school robotics club project.  "Clyde" (not pictured) was our very first attempt, a simple learning platform. Then came "Robotank" (Sherman chassis with a miniboard), "RobotTankII" (Sherman Chassis with 6.270 board), and "Comet" (not pictured, R/C conversion w/Miniboard). We experimented with different designs like "Ghost" (not pictured), a Sumo robot with a miniboard, and "Sherman" pictured with a rug Warrior board.  "Cougar" (pictured with Miniboard) was a popular design, and we also built "Dino" and "ARBE" around the same time.  We're always looking for ways to make robotics more accessible, so we're currently working on "RoboTankIII" (current project), a more affordable and user-friendly robot for the club members to build.

--Chuck

subject: Re:  Backtracking in Icon
body:
Subject: Re:  Backtracking in Icon

Body:

I'm writing to clarify some points about backtracking in Icon, which can be a bit confusing.  

The recent article in the Icon Analyst focused on static analysis techniques, which examine the structure of Icon programs themselves.  This is important for understanding how Icon programs are designed, but it doesn't tell us how they actually behave when they're running.

Future issues of the Analyst will delve into dynamic analysis, which looks at how Icon programs execute.  We'll explore fascinating things like how often expressions are evaluated multiple times to explore different possibilities and achieve the desired outcome.

I hope this helps shed some light on the different ways we can analyze Icon programs!

Best regards,

Ralph E. Griswold			ralph@cs.arizona.edu
Department of Computer Science 		 uunet!arizona!ralph

I'm working on a project that involves a lot of system administration tasks, and I'm trying to learn more about Plan 9. I've been reading the TFM, but I'm still a bit stuck on some basic commands.

Could you help me out with a few things?

* What are the Plan 9 equivalents of commands like 'shutdown', 'halt', and 'sync'? I need to know how to safely shut down the system.
* Also, I'm curious about how to manage processes in Plan 9. What are the commands that correspond to ^C, ^Q, and ^S in other systems? I'm particularly interested in how to pause and resume text output.

Any guidance you could offer would be greatly appreciated!

Thanks,
Volker

subject: Re: [Python-3000] Enough with the u"string" already!
body:
I haven't read that thread yet (and probably never will), but I want to make sure we're being as user-friendly as possible.  To avoid any confusion for new Python developers, I'm proposing that we prioritize clear and consistent syntax in Python 3.0.  

This means we'll be phasing out certain features that are considered outdated or less efficient, like the `u"string"` syntax.  We'll provide clear warnings and documentation to help developers transition to the modern `.format()` method, which is more powerful and flexible.  

The only exception to this will be the `%` formatting, which will remain supported for now to ensure compatibility with existing code.

I believe this approach will ultimately lead to a cleaner and more intuitive Python experience for everyone.

--Guido

subject: [Spambayes] stack.pop() ate my multipart message
body:
Subject: [Spambayes] stack.pop() ate my multipart message

I've been working on improving the performance of hammie, my spam filtering system, and I've noticed a curious issue with multipart/alternative messages. It seems that the way these messages are handled by the tokenizer can lead to unexpected results.

For example, when processing a message with both text and HTML parts, the tokenizer sometimes removes the HTML content entirely. This is likely due to a subtle interaction between the way generators and lists are used in the tokenizer.

I've managed to work around this issue with a temporary patch, but I'm still trying to understand the underlying cause.  I'm hoping that someone with more expertise in Python's internals might be able to shed some light on this.

In the meantime, users who rely on multipart/alternative messages should be aware of this potential issue and consider applying the attached patch as a workaround.


------------------------------------------------------------------
Index: tokenizer.py 
================================================================= 
RCS file: /cvsroot/spambayes/spambayes/tokenizer.py,v retrieving revision 1.15 
diff -u -r1.15 tokenizer

subject: [Spambayes] timtest broke?
body:
Subject: [Spambayes] timtest broke?

Email body:

Hi team,

I was just experimenting with some new features for Spambayes and ran into a little snag.  It seems the `ConfigParser` library might be behaving unexpectedly.  I noticed that if a file named 'bayes.ini' isn't found, it doesn't throw an error, which could lead to some confusion.  

To make things clearer and more robust, I've decided to move the configuration options directly into the `Options.py` file. This eliminates the need for a separate 'bayes.ini' file, so you won't have to worry about search path issues anymore.

For those who want to customize the tokenizer and testers, I've created a new file called 'bayescustomize.ini'.  Just keep in mind that you'll need to make sure this file is in the correct location.  

Let me know if you have any questions!

subject: Backtracking in Icon
body:
Subject: Backtracking in Icon

An article in the latest "Icon Analyst" reported frequency of use
of various control structures in Icon programs, but backtracking
was mentioned only briefly.  When I first learned about
backtracking and goal seeking behavior in Icon I thought,
"Wow.  Now I can have backtracking like I had in Prolog, AND
I don't have to try to write loops and if statements in
screwy ways."

I was really excited to explore this powerful new feature and
experiment with its potential in my Icon projects.  However,
after some experimentation, I've found that I don't use it as
much as I initially anticipated.  It seems that for many common
tasks, simpler constructs like:

    (checkValid(x) | write("error"))

are sufficient.  While I understand the elegance of backtracking,
I'm wondering if it's perhaps more of a niche feature, best
suited for very specific problem domains.

I'm curious to hear from other Icon programmers about their
experiences with backtracking.  Do you find it to be a valuable
tool in your daily work, or is it something you primarily
use for educational purposes?

subject: Aus der Vi ..
body:
This is a fascinating account of my diplomatic efforts to maintain peace and stability in Europe.  

As you can see, I was deeply concerned about the growing tensions between nations, particularly the increasing military buildup by Russia. I sought to understand the motivations behind these actions and to find ways to de-escalate the situation.

My conversations with various individuals, including Ballin and von Bethmann, highlight the complexities of international relations.  We discussed the importance of historical context and the need to consider the unique circumstances of each nation.  

I also met with Admiral von Tirpitz to discuss the crucial role of a strong navy in safeguarding our national interests.  It was clear that the security of Germany depended on a robust defense posture.

My interactions with King Edward VII's emissary, Haldane, were particularly insightful. While I respected him as a man of principle, I felt he lacked the necessary experience for his position.

Throughout these endeavors, I remained committed to diplomacy and peaceful solutions.  However, I also recognized the need for Germany to be prepared to defend itself against any potential threats.

subject: Re: [ILUG] SUSE 8 disks? (thread changed slightly)
body:
Subject: Re: [ILUG] SUSE 8 disks? (thread changed slightly)

Body:

Hi everyone,

I was just having a friendly discussion with Paul about the GNU GPL and how it works.  It seems there's some confusion about whether you can charge for GPL software.  Paul was explaining that while you can technically charge for binaries, you have to provide the source code for free if someone asks.  

He gave a really interesting example about selling a compiled version of /bin/true for a high price, but then having to give the source code away for free if someone wanted it.  He made a good point about how the GPL encourages people to add value to GPL software beyond just providing the binaries, like offering convenient installers or support.

It's great to see such a lively discussion about open source licensing!

Best,

Kenn

subject: Re[3]: Selling Wedded Bliss (was Re: Ouch...)
body:
In addition, one interesting anecdote from a conversation in 1984 in San Francisco highlights a common misconception about promiscuity. 

This quote, which I found relating to societal perceptions of risk, illustrates how people often downplay their own behavior compared to others:

"I think people feel a certain invulnerability, especially young people, like this disease doesn't affect me. The publicity about the disease was very much the kind where it was easy to say, "That isn't me. I'm not promiscuous." Promiscuity, especially, was a piece where people could easily say, "Well, I'm not. Promiscuous is more than I do." If you have 300 partners a year, you can think you're not promiscuous if you know somebody who has 500. So it's all relative, and it was easy to feel that that isn't me."

It's important to remember that this kind of relative comparison can be misleading.  We should strive for accurate and honest self-assessment rather than relying on comparisons to others.

This anecdote reminds us to be mindful of our own behaviors and to avoid making generalizations about entire groups of people.

-BB

subject: 615 PAPER 1
body:
This document outlines the inner workings of several routing algorithms commonly used in ad-hoc networks. These algorithms, DSDV, DSR, and AODV, are essential for enabling communication in dynamic, decentralized environments where network topology constantly changes.

The document begins by explaining DSDV, a distance-vector algorithm that efficiently handles frequent topology changes. It highlights the use of sequence numbers to prevent routing loops and the dynamic computation of "route settling time" to minimize network flooding.

Next, the document delves into DSR, a source routing algorithm that leverages on-demand route discovery to reduce control overhead. It explains how DSR utilizes cached routes and promiscuous mode to optimize routing information dissemination. The document also discusses the challenges DSR faces in partitioned networks and the back-off mechanism employed to mitigate them.

Finally, the document introduces AODV, a hybrid algorithm that combines the strengths of DSDV and DSR. It emphasizes AODV's efficient use of memory, its ability to scale to large networks, and its robust handling of broken links and route changes.

The document concludes by showcasing the performance of AODV through simulations, demonstrating its effectiveness in achieving high goodput ratios, low bandwidth overhead, and manageable latencies.

subject: Re: How to "source" another perl-file into one
body:

> Hi Users,
> 
> I'm working on a really cool project that involves combining different Perl scripts for increased efficiency. I want to "source" another file (also a Perl script) into my main script at the beginning. There are some helpful definitions and functions in this other file that I need to load before running the rest of my script. I know how to do this in shell scripts using ".", but I'm not sure how to achieve the same thing in Perl.
> 
> Any suggestions or guidance would be greatly appreciated!

Modules is what you are after.

create your subroutines or definitions that you want to use in a file
called xyz.pm  (xyz should be something meaningful)

use lib '/where/my/module/is/';
use xyz

subject: On the ARL Server (Dec. 19, 2005)
body:
Subject: On the ARL Server (Dec. 19, 2005)

Body:

This week, the Association of Research Libraries (ARL) is highlighting some exciting developments in the world of academic research and library services.  

We're celebrating the recent passage of the CURES Bill, which is expected to significantly improve access to vital biomedical research.  

The ARL has also released several valuable resources, including SPEC Kit 290 on Access Services and ARL Statistics 2003-04.  

In addition, we're featuring an insightful interview with SPARC Executive Director Heather Joseph, discussing the future of scholarly communication.  

Don't miss the upcoming ARL events at the ALA Midwinter Meeting in San Antonio, including a forum on authors' rights and copyright.  

We encourage you to register for LibQUAL+ workshops and learn how to improve your library's services.  

Finally, mark your calendars for the Living the Future 6 conference, where we'll explore the latest trends in library technology and innovation.

Kaylyn Hipps

subject: Re: Problem ordering the distribution
body:
>	Why don't you just buy it directly from USA? I bought it
> from Spain with my credit card and had no problems.

I've been trying to get my hands on Plan 9 for ages! It's such a unique operating system, and I'm really excited to try it out.  I finally managed to order it from the USA last August, even though I live in England.  It was a bit of a hassle, but I got it in the end.  I called their 800 number and they quoted me a price of $350 plus $29.40 for expedited shipping.  I sent them a cheque and waited patiently.

A month later, I called to check on the status of my order, and they said they couldn't find any record of it!  Can you believe that?  Luckily, I had kept a copy of the cancelled cheque, so I sent that to them.  Finally, they sent me the CDROM and manuals, along with an invoice for $378.50.  I also had to pay some extra fees for shipping and taxes, but it was worth it to get my hands on Plan 9.

subject: Digital input cct
body:
Hi there,

I'm working on a fun project building a custom control panel for my home theater setup. I'm at the stage where I'm trying to integrate digital input functionality, and I've hit a snag.

I'm on step 7.2 (Digital Input Circuit) and the testdigitals(); function in hbtest.c isn't responding to the start button. I've double-checked all the components and connections, and everything seems to be in place.  

Interestingly, when I test pin1 of U5, the yellow Charge LED on the I/C board lights up brightly. I'm not sure if this is normal behavior, as it seems a bit unusual.  It's even happening even when the testdigitals(); function isn't running, and it seems to be interfering with the communication between the panel and my computer.

This project is a personal challenge for me, and I've been stuck on this for a while now. Any advice or insights would be greatly appreciated!

Thanks,
Rob O' Neill

subject: 320p 4/9 Update: Soyuz docks with station
body:
03:20 p.m., 04/09/07, Update: Soyuz docks with space station

A Russian Soyuz spacecraft carrying two fresh crew members and a wealthy American space tourist docked with the international space station today to close out a two-day orbital chase. The linkup at the downward facing port of the Russian Zarya module occurred at 3:10 p.m. as the two spacecraft sailed high above Eastern Europe.

On board the Soyuz TMA-10 were spacecraft commander Oleg Kotov, Expedition 15 commander Fyodor Yurchikhin and U.S. software developer Charles Simonyi, who paid the Russians more than $20 million to become the fifth private citizen to visit the space station. This historic event marks a new era of space tourism, with more private citizens expected to follow in Simonyi's footsteps.

"Contact and capture, docking confirmed, at 11:10 p.m. Moscow time (3:10 p.m. EDT)," said NASA commentator Rob Navias from the Russian mission control center. "Docking confirmed, a perfect arrival. Soyuz TMA-10 has arrived at the international space station."

subject: [Spambayes] Ditching WordInfo
body:
Subject: [Spambayes] Ditching WordInfo

[Neale Pickett] > ... >  I've been experimenting with different ways to improve the performance of Spambayes, and I've found that using the pickle store can be quite beneficial. It only needs to write to disk once, which can save a lot of time in the long run.  Of course, it does require a bit more memory, but the performance gains are worth it. I'm always looking for ways to make Spambayes faster and more efficient, but I'm also trying to be mindful of resource usage.  Profiling the code is a possibility, but it's a bit time-consuming.  I'm hoping that the current approach is already optimized enough for most users.  

Guido has been very helpful in testing and providing feedback on these changes. He's always eager to see improvements, and he's been particularly interested in the performance gains we've seen with the pickle store.

Email body:

Russ Cox (rsc@pan9.bell-labs.com) suggested I try some troubleshooting steps for my old computer. He recommended a specific command to check the hard drive and floppy drive compatibility.

I ran the command and it seems like there might be a problem with my floppy drive. The output indicated it couldn't access the device.  

I'm not very familiar with the internal components of this older Intel system, so I'm hoping to identify the chip responsible for handling the floppy drive.  I'll need to open it up and check the bus speeds to see if that's the issue.

As a temporary solution, I'm going to try connecting the floppy drive to a different controller, an AHA1540/42CP SCSI controller, to see if that resolves the problem.


Thanks for the advice, Russ!

subject: OWB Articles: Important Article.
body:
Subject: OWB Articles: Important Article.

This is a collection of interesting and thought-provoking phrases and ideas.  It's a bit abstract, but I think it speaks to the complexities of modern life and the interconnectedness of seemingly disparate things.  

For example, the juxtaposition of "a turn signal toward the tornado" and "a flavored hell" suggests the unpredictable nature of our world, while the image of "a football team beams with joy" evokes a sense of shared happiness and community. 

The piece also touches on themes of power and influence, with references to "a CEO" and "an industrial complex."  It seems to be suggesting that these forces can be both positive and negative, capable of both great good and great harm.

Ultimately, this is a piece that invites the reader to think critically and creatively about the world around them.

subject: Type conversion.
body:
Subject: Type conversion.

Hi to all,

I'm working on a fun project where I'm simulating the movement of celestial bodies.  I'm still learning about programming concepts like data types.

I'm trying to figure out how to convert an integer number representing the distance between two planets to a floating point number so I can accurately calculate the gravitational force between them.  I tried this:

          int temp;
          float dtemp;

         dtemp=temp*3.5;

But when I try to run my simulation, I get an error message about incompatible types.

Can anyone help me understand what I'm doing wrong?

Thanks in advance!

Federico Santello

subject: Re: [R] [R-sig-Geo] LIDAR Problem in R (THANKS for HELP)
body: Let's not cross-post!  I'll pick up only one point of general relevance on  this list (R-help). On Tue, 5 Aug 2008, Matt Oliver wrote: > you can try > > memory.limit(size=4000) > > only if you have 4GB of memory on the system > > This is not guaranteed to solve your problem though Assuming this is Windows (it looks like it) ....  The limiting issue on a  32-bit system is not memory (RAM) but address space: using  memory.limit(size=4000) will still only get you 2GB of address space  unless you use the /3GB flag.  (See the rw-FAQ Q2.9.) > With big datasets like lidar, you are much better off getting access to a > 64bit system with a ton of RAM (>64GB). Well, you are better off even with 4GB of RAM because of address-space  issues (and even with 2GB if the 32-bit OS is Windows -- I used to run a  dual-boot Windows XP / 64-bit Linux box with

body:
> Who loses in this case? Why? Neither party is inaccurate in their
> actions IMO. But someone's standing is going to get hurt because of it.

My question for the trust system is this. I'm trying to help a friend who signed up for a free account on a website that offers a wealth of information.  They're excited about the new articles and books they can access, and they even get a few links to their bookstore.

However, their email provider keeps flagging these emails as spam, even though my friend explicitly agreed to receive them and actually enjoys reading the content.  They even have an option to unsubscribe if they change their mind!

It seems unfair that these legitimate emails are being misclassified as spam.  Who is losing a trust rating here? If it's not unsolicited commercial email, shouldn't it be treated as legitimate by the email system?

--Will

subject: [Spambayes] test sets?
body:
Subject: [Spambayes] test sets?

Hi Guido,

I've been playing around with the classifier pickle you shared on SourceForge. It's really impressive! It even caught a spam message that slipped past my usual spam filter.

However, I noticed it's flagging some legitimate emails as spam. Specifically, anything with MIME structure or HTML parts seems to be getting caught, including some messages from my "zope geeks" list.  

I'm wondering if there's a reason for this.  It seems like it might be overly sensitive to HTML, even when it's part of a multipart/alternative message with a text/plain part.  

I'm thinking maybe it's because the HTML isn't being stripped properly.  Would it be possible to add an option to the tokenizer to strip HTML tags even if the message is pure HTML? That would be really helpful.

I'm eager to see if retraining the classifier with a more diverse dataset will improve its accuracy.  I'm curious to compare its performance to Jeremy's, who mentioned having some higher error rates.


Let me know your thoughts!

Best,

[Your Name]

subject: [Spambayes] Ditching WordInfo
body:
Subject: [Spambayes] Ditching WordInfo

Hey everyone,

So I was chatting with Tim Peters about some performance optimizations I've been working on for Spambayes.  He suggested a different approach to storing WordInfo data that could potentially save some memory.  

Right now, I'm storing individual WordInfo pickles in an anydbm dictionary for super fast lookups when scoring messages. It's made a huge difference in speed, almost 50 times faster!  But Tim pointed out that this might not be the most memory-efficient way to do things.

I'm always looking for ways to improve Spambayes, so I'm going to explore Tim's suggestion and see if I can find a way to make it even better.  Maybe I'll even write a subclass to handle the memory management more efficiently.  

Gotta keep things running smoothly!

Neale

subject: Re: Downloading
body:
Subject: Re: Downloading 

OK, attempting to answer this one now.

It seems like you're having some trouble getting your handy board to communicate with your Mac.  Let's try a few things to see if we can get it working.

I'm thinking it might be a problem with the serial port on your Mac, not the handy board itself.

Try this: download a program called Zterm (a terminal emulator) from:

  http://hyperarchive.lcs.mit.edu/HyperArchive/Archive/comm/term/zterm-101.hqx

Then, connect to the printer port on your Mac (you can do this by holding down the shift key while it boots up). Set your connection speed to 300 baud. Now, just type some random characters on your keyboard.  If the green light on the handy board's serial interface flashes, that means data is getting through. If not, there might be a problem with your Mac's hardware or software settings.

Let me know if you see the green light, and we'll go from there.

fred

subject: Re: Increasing current output to motors on HandyBoard
body:
Subject: Re: Increasing current output to motors on HandyBoard

Body:

Hi Ian,

Just saw your question about boosting the current to the motors on the HandyBoard.  

I was thinking about the same thing the other day!  Stacking those TI chips might seem like a quick fix, but I wouldn't recommend it.  It's a bit risky and could lead to some overheating issues. 

Instead, have you considered using the TI chips to control some beefier power transistors?  That way, the transistors can handle the extra current without any problems.  

Another option is to swap out those L293 chips for some National LM18200 H-bridges. They're designed to handle higher voltages and currents, so they'd be perfect for your motors.

Let me know if you have any other questions!

Best,

Chuck

subject: [opensuse] Defragging: possible?  necessary?
body:
Subject: [opensuse] Defragging: possible?  necessary?

Email body:

Fred wrote:

I've been doing some research on different file systems and their performance characteristics.  It's interesting to see how different operating systems handle fragmentation.

I've had experience with both Windows and Linux systems, and I've noticed a significant difference in how they manage disk space.  While Windows uses NTFS, which is designed to be more resistant to fragmentation, I've found that even with relatively low disk usage, Windows servers still benefit from regular defragmentation.  

On the other hand, Linux systems using ext3 seem to handle fragmentation much better, even without regular defragmentation.  This suggests that while NTFS might be more resistant to fragmentation, it's not entirely immune, and regular maintenance might still be necessary for optimal performance.

I'm curious to hear other people's experiences with different file systems and their defragmentation needs.

-- 
Fred

subject: [ILUG] Re: Csh shell scripts
body:
> quickie for shell scripters:
> How do I find out the time a file was created at?
 
  You might be surprised to learn that figuring out a file's creation time can be tricky!  There isn't a single, foolproof method, and sometimes the information just isn't available.  

  Let me explain. When you look at how the `stat` structure is defined, you'll see it keeps track of things like the last access time, modification time, and file status change time.  The "file status change" time is actually a catch-all that includes things like creation, changes to permissions, or even ownership changes. So, it's only truly accurate for the creation time if the file hasn't been modified in any other way since it was first created.

  You can get a glimpse of this information using the `ls -lc` command, but it's a bit messy to parse.  A simpler approach is to write a small C program that directly accesses the file's status and displays the "ctime" – that's the time of the last status change, measured in seconds since the beginning of time (the "epoch"). This gives you a precise numerical
