subject: Re: [R] Rserve and R to R communication
body:
Hi Paul,

Thanks for your message, but I'm not 100% clear on your meaning. Could you unpack your logic a bit? Is this because two (+) sub-processes that are begun at precisely the same time will both have the same set.seed value (by default) for any RNG? That seems a bit unlikely, however, you are changing the pattern of the random numbers by mixing numbers from generators on different machines running in parallel. So the numbers you are generating are not according to the studied properties of the generator you are nominally using. People who study random number generation worry about this sort of thing. You would not be able to claim you are using any particular scheme, which would be a problem if you want to rely on the results or publish them.

My guess is that the numbers might be quite random, perhaps even hard to reproduce (which is another problem). But my guess is not something you would want to claim as authoritative.

It's great that you're exploring different ways to parallelize your simulations!  It sounds like you're trying to find the most efficient and reliable method for handling random number generation across multiple processes.  

Let me know if you have any other questions as you work through this.

I tried out a font called "QuickType II" that I found on my Windows partition, and while it works great for non-antialiased rendering, I noticed some issues with the antialiasing.  It might just be a quirk of this particular font, though.

subject: New Ebook: Make Easy and Safe Backups in Leopard
body:
Hi everyone,

We're excited to announce the release of our new ebook, "Take Control of Easy Backups in Leopard"!  

This ebook is a must-have for anyone who wants to ensure their precious data is safe and secure.  We know that Time Machine is a great feature in Leopard, but it's important to have a comprehensive backup strategy that goes beyond just using Time Machine.

In this ebook, our expert Joe Kissell provides a step-by-step guide to creating a complete backup system for your Leopard machine.  He covers everything from archiving to creating a bootable duplicate, and even how to back up your data offsite.  

We've also included some helpful tips and tricks that aren't found in Apple's documentation, as well as information on alternative backup solutions for those times when Time Machine might not be the best option.

Because we want to make sure everyone has access to this valuable information as soon as possible, we're releasing the ebook in preview form.  This means you can start learning about backup best practices right away!  

The preview includes a three-page PDF with a special link to access a web-based preview of the full ebook.  You can also use this link

subject: [DMDX] Display Timing issue?
body:
Subject: [DMDX] Display Timing issue?

Hi,

I'm working on a fascinating project using DMDX to create a visual display for a scientific experiment.  I'm trying to achieve very precise timing, showing images for a single frame and then triggering a sensor based on which image was displayed.

I've made good progress, but I'm encountering some unexpected behavior.  After displaying the first two images correctly, the system seems to freeze on the third image, showing a white screen.  Additionally, I've noticed a slight delay between when the sensor output changes and when the image actually appears on the screen.

I'm using a relatively old computer system (2GHz P4, 256MB RAM) with an nVidia GeForce4 MX420 graphics card. I've tried different images (white, black, and a custom image) but the issue persists.

I'm hoping someone with more experience with DMDX can offer some guidance on how to improve the timing synchronization. Any suggestions would be greatly appreciated!

Thanks,

[Your Name]

subject: Re: Microsoft buys XDegress - more of a p2p/distributed data thing...
body:
Mr. FoRK writes:

> "Files can be cached on multiple systems randomly scattered around the
> Internet, as with Napster or Freenet. In fact, the caching in XDegrees is
> more sophisticated than it is on those systems: users with high bandwidth
> connections can download portions, or "stripes," of a file from several
> cached locations simultaneously. The XDegrees software then reassembles
> these stripes into the whole file and uses digital signatures to verify that
> the downloaded file is the same as the original. A key component of this
> digital signature is a digest of the file, which is stored as an HTTP header
> for the file."

This "more sophisticated than [Napster or Freenet]" part seems
to be the same behavior implemented in many other P2P **content sharing** platforms, 
such as:

  - Kazaa
  - EDonkey/Overnet
  - BitTorrent
  - Gnutella (with HUGE extensions)
  - OnionNetworks WebRAID

...though the quality of the "digest" used by each system varies
wildly.

It's interesting to see how XDegrees is building upon these existing technologies to create a

subject: Re: [Webdev] PHP+Javascript request
body:
> er, I understood you until you reached the above. Maybe it's my lack of 
> PHP but why would a variable name _need_ to end with [] ?
> 
> With most languages you parse the submitted form data and should come 
> out with either variables or an array / hash, which you then work with 
> and name whatever you like.

I was just thinking about how to make our website more user-friendly.  I've been experimenting with different ways to handle multiple selections in forms, and I came across this interesting technique in PHP. 

IIRC, if the query string contains

varname=FIRST&varname=SECOND

then PHP will create a variable called "varname" with result SECOND. 
However, if the query string contains

varname[]=FIRST&varname[]=SECOND

then PHP will create an array with varname[1]==FIRST and varname[2]==SECOND

This seems like a clever way to store multiple selections in a single variable, making it easier to process later.  I'm still exploring the best approach, but this PHP method seems promising.

Dave


_______________________________________________
Webdev mailing list
Webdev@linux.

This is a compilation of recent discussions on the Weka machine learning forum, showcasing the collaborative spirit and expertise within the community. 

Today's Topics:

1. **Adding a new Filter---Jar generation (Cristina Torres):** Cristina successfully compiled Weka and introduces a new filter. She's now working on packaging it into a .jar file and seeks advice on the proper generation process.

2. **gridregression.py (M. Fatih Akay):**  M. Fatih Akay explores the possibility of integrating the gridregression.py tool from LIBSVM into Weka for optimizing parameters. This highlights the ongoing efforts to enhance Weka's capabilities.

subject: Re: Difference between designer and OEM Polariod kit
body:
Peter Eacmen wrote:
> What's the difference between the Designer and OEM polaroid kits.  > Which one do I need to hook up the polaroid sensors to the HB?

The OEM kit is a great starting point for anyone interested in exploring the potential of Polaroid sensors with the HB. It provides the essential components - two transducers and two ranging circuit boards - at a very affordable price.  

The Designer kit, on the other hand, is geared towards more advanced users who want to delve deeper into the technical aspects. It includes all the components of the OEM kit, plus a bonus experimental demonstration board. This board allows you to see the raw data from the sensors in a visual format, which can be incredibly helpful for understanding how the sensors work and for experimenting with different applications.

While the HB itself does have some display capabilities, the demonstration board offers a more interactive and in-depth visualization experience.  It's a valuable tool for anyone serious about pushing the boundaries of what's possible with Polaroid sensors and the HB.

Just a heads-up, prices can vary depending on the retailer.  Some places, like Wirz Electronics, offer competitive prices on individual components, allowing you to start small and expand your setup as needed.  Others

subject: [Spambayes] understanding high false negative rate
body:
>>> Jeremy Hylton wrote > Then I tried a dirt simple tokenizer for the headers that tokenize the > words in the header and emitted like this "%s: %s" % (hdr, word). > That worked too well :-).  The received and date headers helped the > classifier discover that most of my spam is old and most of my ham is > new. Heh. I hit the same problem, but the other way round, when I first started playing with this - I'd collected spam for a week or two,  then mixed it up with randomly selected messages from my mail boxes. course, it instantly picked up on 'received:2001' as a non-ham.  Curse that too-smart-for-me software.  I'm really impressed with how well this software can learn patterns! It's amazing how it can identify spam based on seemingly insignificant details like the date header.  Still, it's probably a good thing to note in the documentation about the software - when collecting spam/ham, make _sure_ you try and collect from the same source. Anthony --  Anthony Baxter         It's never too late to have a happy childhood.

subject: HABEAS_ACCREDITED_COI
body:
Hi,

I've been working on improving our spam filtering system and have noticed a strange issue.  We've been receiving a number of emails that seem to be bypassing our filters.  These emails appear to be related to a company called HABEAS, and they're targeting specific, unused email addresses within our system.

I've checked their website, but I can't find any clear information on how to report these types of issues or even verify the legitimacy of the company.  They do have a form for requesting removal from their mailing list, but that doesn't address the core problem – the fact that these emails are getting through our filters in the first place.

I'm concerned about the potential security implications of this.  I'm currently considering adjusting the scoring system for emails flagged with the HABEAS_ACCREDITED_??? rules to make them neutral. This would prevent them from being automatically blocked, but it would also mean we need to investigate each one manually.

Has anyone else encountered similar issues with HABEAS or have any suggestions on how to handle this situation?

-- 
Anthony Peacock
CHIME, Royal Free & University College Medical School
WWW:    http://www.chime.ucl.

subject: Re: C++ Standardization of intervals
body:
reliable_computing subscribers et al,

We've been exploring the potential of interval data types, and we've learned a lot from our experience with Fortran. While there was initial support for an interval data type within the Fortran standardization committee (J3), we ultimately encountered some technical challenges that prevented us from reaching a consensus.  

However, we believe these technical hurdles have been overcome, and we're now in a much stronger position to advocate for the benefits of interval data types.  We're confident that intervals offer significant advantages for a wider range of applications beyond just specialized use cases.

We're excited about the future of intervals and believe they have the potential to become a valuable tool for the broader programming community.  

Sincerely,

Baker

P.S.  While Fortran has its merits, we see intervals potentially having a longer-lasting impact on the programming landscape.

subject: Dumb math question related to Navigation and encoders
body:
Subject: Dumb math question related to Navigation and encoders

Email body:

Hi everyone,

I'm working on a fun project using Handyboard Interactive C to simulate a simple robot navigation system.  I'm trying to figure out how to calculate things like sine, cosine, tangent, and arctangent for my dead reckoning calculations. 

I'm new to Interactive C and not sure if there's a built-in library for these trigonometric functions or if I need to figure out the formulas myself.  

Has anyone else tackled this before?  Maybe you have a function library or a helpful resource that could point me in the right direction?  Any guidance would be greatly appreciated!

subject: Re: [opensuse] Defragging: possible?  necessary?
body:
jdd wrote:
> Carlos E. R. a écrit :
> 
>> The question for XP admins is if that task is really worthwhile; I 
>> mean, if having a computer off-line for several hours with heavy disk 
>> activity and wear, as compared to been perhaps a small percent slower 
>> in normal use.
> 
> luch worse than that.  I've noticed that the swap file can become fragmented on XP, and this can really slow down the computer.  Unfortunately, it can't be defragmented directly! So, the only solution is to temporarily disable the swap file, restart the computer, delete it manually, defragment the hard drive, and then reset the swap file. It's a bit of a hassle.
> 
> (I had to do this on my own XP machine a month ago... It's a pain!)
> 
> jdd

So FreeBSD's ipfw is not encumbered in the same way the old > OBSD firewalling was? Nope, indeed IPFW2 has just been rolled out into -STABLE. (-STABLE is a stable branch of the code that has been rolled into -CURRENT first. It's basically a major release, that's still a work in progress) > to an extent, i guess so, yes. but i've a few boxes with reasonable  > uptimes that run netfilter/iptables. (i've one that has crashed twice  > now after 60+ day uptimes. but that doesnt seem to be netfilter). >  > course, there's a lot more that can go wrong with a firewall than the > firewall code. in that case get 2 boxes and heartbeat them. I don't really judge things by uptimes, it's rather like judging penis size. People like comparing them, and the larger they are, the more people are in awe of them, but overly large

subject: [DMDX] RE: Re: DmDX script automation
body:
>Plus, while we've been able to teach undergrads how to program a fully functional and publishable E-Prime program in a couple of weeks, we had to go through three undergrads over a year to get a DMDX study up and running (and while they understood the fundamentals of E-Prime programming, they never got the hang of what DMDX was all about in the couple of months they worked on it individually).

I've actually found DMDX to be quite user-friendly!  I think the language is very intuitive, especially for beginners.  For example, explaining what a "refresh rate" is and how it controls timing was a breeze for my students.  I even had a first-year undergraduate class, who were admittedly not the most tech-savvy, write a simple lexical decision experiment in just a few weeks.  Of course, I did simplify things a bit by focusing on the core concepts and avoiding some of the more complex aspects, like I/O cards.  But even with that, they were able to grasp the basics quickly and create a functional experiment.

- Mike

body:
Hi All,

Get ready for some friendly competition! On Saturday, March 25, 2000, from 3-5pm, Penn State Abington College is hosting a fun and exciting Fire-Fighting Robot Contest.  

This event is inspired by the amazing International Fire-Fighting Robot Contest held annually at Trinity College, but with a more relaxed and accessible twist!  Teams will guide their robots through a maze with four rooms, searching for a candle and putting it out as quickly as possible. We've made some adjustments to make it even more enjoyable, like removing the penalty for bumping into walls and adding a special exhibition for teams who want to showcase their unique creations.

Whether you're a seasoned robotics enthusiast or just curious about this fascinating field, everyone is welcome to join the fun!  It's a great opportunity to learn, network, and maybe even win some prizes (details to be announced soon).  Plus, who doesn't love free pizza?

subject: Re: Recommended Viewing
body:
On 10 Sep 2002, Gary Lawrence Murphy wrote:

--]It's a myth that we don't use parts of our brain.  We use it all,
--]always.  It's just that our culturally-induced focal-point causes most
--]of us to most of the time ignore and waste 99.999% of it.  "Lucid" is
--]a measure of notch-filter bandwidth; all stations are broadcasting,
--]but we only /choose/ Easy Rock 105.
--]
--]For example, don't look now but your shoes are full of feet.

That's a fascinating way to think about it!  It's like we're all tuning into different frequencies, focusing on specific aspects of reality while ignoring the rest.  It makes you wonder what else we might be missing out on.  

It's amazing how much is hidden beneath the surface, just waiting to be discovered.  Like a treasure map, our minds hold countless secrets just waiting to be unlocked.

Life, the ultimate adventure!

-tom

subject: [Razor-users] Smrazor, Milter, and Max connections
body:
Subject: [Razor-users] Smrazor, Milter, and Max connections

Hi everyone,

I'm working on a really cool project using Razor v2.14 and Solaris 9 with sendmail 8.12.5. I'm trying to implement smrazor as a milter to enhance security and spam filtering.  It's working great for light traffic, but I'm running into some issues during peak times.

I'm seeing the smrazor milter occasionally stop responding and even crash.  I've set a 15-second timeout per message, but I'm getting a lot of timeouts clustered together, and sometimes I get the "Could not get valid info from Discovery Servers" error.

I'm not using procmail because this server acts as a gateway for five different POP3 servers, each with its own domain.  Directly using procmail wouldn't work because of overlapping usernames across the domains.

Has anyone else encountered similar issues with smrazor under heavy load?  Do you know if there's a theoretical limit on the number of messages per minute it can handle?

I'm also open to suggestions for other high-capacity milters or alternative solutions for

subject: Re-Compliments From Mr Turkin & Monica
body:
Dear Sir,

I hope this email finds you well. My name is Turkin Eze, and I'm writing to you today with a story of resilience and hope. I'm a refugee from Sierra Leone, currently living in Dakar, Senegal, with my younger sister, Monica.

Tragically, my father, Chief Dr. O. ONUORAH, was a prominent figure in Sierra Leone's gold and diamond mining industry. He was deeply respected and dedicated to his work.  Unfortunately, he was caught in the crossfire of a political crisis and lost his life during a violent raid on his office.  

The turmoil forced us to flee our home, leaving behind everything we knew. We were fortunate to be helped by a kind army colonel, a close friend of my father, who guided us to safety in Senegal.

Despite the immense loss and hardship we've endured, we remain determined to rebuild our lives. We yearn for the opportunity to continue our education and pursue our dreams.

Recently, I discovered that my father had wisely invested a portion of his savings in a secure foreign financial institution. This unexpected discovery offers a glimmer of hope for our future.

I'm reaching out to you because I believe your expertise and connections could be invaluable

subject: More Motor Controller Question
body:
Subject: More Motor Controller Question

Email body:

Hi Fred,

Thanks again for your help! I'm making good progress with the motor controller.  I've been experimenting with setting values in $7000 and I can see the corresponding input pin on the l293 react.  

However, I've noticed something strange.  The pin doesn't seem to hold the position I set it to. It quickly flickers back to its initial state.  It's almost like there's some kind of automatic calibration happening that's overriding my input.  

Do you know if there's a way to temporarily disable this automatic calibration feature?  I'm hoping there's a software setting I can tweak to prevent this from happening while I'm testing.

Thanks,

Bruce Moore
bamoore@bloomington.in.us

subject: Re: Sorting dir output
body:
Subject: Re: Sorting dir output

Hello,

Craig Schneider wrote:
> Hi Guys

> How could I exec a 'dir' command on a dos system and put the output in
> an array, sort by date and the files that are older than 3 days be moved
> into a folder called 'history'


I was trying to help Craig with a script to organize his files. He wanted to automatically move files older than three days into a separate folder called "history".  I provided him with a Perl script that does just that. 

Here's the code:

# open the current directory
opendir my $dh, '.' or die "Cannot open '.' $!";

# get files older than three days
my @files = grep -M > 3, readdir $dh;

closedir $dh;

for my $file ( @files ) {
    rename $file, "history/$file" or die "Cannot move '$file' $!";
    }




John
-- 
Perl isn't a toolbox, but a small machine shop where you can special-order
certain sorts of tools at low cost and in short order.       -- Larry Wall

-- 
To unsubscribe,

subject: Need Help - 68HC11E1/68HC24 and 32K external RAM
body:
Subject: Need Help - 68HC11E1/68HC24 and 32K external RAM

Hi everyone,

I'm working on a really cool project for a robotics competition! I've got this awesome hardware board with an E1 processor and a 68HC24 microcontroller, along with 32K of external RAM. I'm using it to build a base station for our robot team.

The board came with some basic documentation, but I'm having trouble figuring out how to fully utilize the 68HC24's capabilities.  I've been reading the Motorola reference manual ("The Pink Bible") and it mentions something about the 68HC24 handling Ports B and C for memory addressing, but I'm not sure how to implement that.

I've managed to get the RugWarrior pcode working to control the motor ports (porta and porte), but I can't seem to access Ports B and C.

Has anyone else worked with this combination of hardware before? Any advice or insights would be greatly appreciated!

Thanks,
Ranjit

subject: Re: Rule for Russian character sets
body:
Subject: Re: Rule for Russian character sets

KB>  I was just thinking about how we can make our security protocols more efficient.  It seems like focusing on specific character sets might not be the best way to go.  

Think about it like this: if we tried to flag everyone who uses a specific language, like Russian, wouldn't that be incredibly cumbersome?  We'd have to constantly update a massive list of names and potentially miss important security threats.  

We've discussed this before, and I think it's important to remember that a more comprehensive approach is needed.  Let's keep exploring other options.

>  ...
>  Can you give another rule that you prefer and that I can comprehend and
>  that I can explain to Python newcomers?  The OP only gave a few examples of
>  * usage and (initially) no rule that I saw.

Let me illustrate the power and flexibility of Python's * operator with a fun analogy! Imagine you have a magical box that can expand things.  

This box, represented by the * symbol, can take various inputs and create bigger, more complex outputs. For example, "3*5" is like asking the box to multiply 3 by 5, resulting in a larger number. Similarly, "3**5" is like asking the box to expand 3 five times, leading to exponentiation.

The * operator can also be used with lists and modules.  Think of "import * from mod" as asking the box to bring all the contents of module "mod" into your current workspace. It's like unpacking a treasure chest filled with useful tools!

Now, let's say you have a list named "a

I'm really excited about using DMDX! I've been working on a fascinating experiment and have mostly figured out the script. I'm using a Compaq Presario 2800 laptop, and I've noticed something peculiar.  

While the touchpad and USB mouse work perfectly fine in all other programs, they seem to "sleep" when I run my DMDX experiment.  The SpaceBar, however, works flawlessly. I've double-checked my script for errors, but I'm not sure if there's something I've missed. My laptop has some Chinese button labels, and I had to figure out the corresponding numbers for the input devices during the TimeDX input test.  

Here's a snippet of my script:

0 "Press SPACEBAR to start."; +1  / *   "sun" /  "sun" /  / ;  -2  / *   "lip"/  "book" /  / ;  -3  / *   "banana"/  "apple" /

subject: Re: [R] nlm() and optim()
body:
It's great that you're exploring different optimization methods in R!  Reproducibility is indeed crucial, especially when comparing results across different software environments like MATLAB and R. 

You're right to be cautious about the potential for variations in results, even with seemingly straightforward equations.  Numerical optimization algorithms can be sensitive to initial parameter values and the specific implementation details.  

It's fantastic that you're aiming to visualize the likelihood surface. This can provide valuable insights into the shape of the optimization landscape and help you understand how different methods perform.  

Remember, the goal is not necessarily to find the absolute global minimum, but rather to find a solution that is sufficiently close to it for your specific application.  


Let me know if you have any more questions as you delve deeper into this!

subject: [UAI] CFP: LCC'06 - Workshop on Logic and Computational Complexity
body:
CALL FOR PAPERS: 8th Intl Workshop on Logic and Computational Complexity
------------------------------------------------------------------------

  The Logic and Computational Complexity Workshop, LCC'06, will be 
  part of the 2006 Federated Logic Conference, FLoC'06, and
  satellite workshop of the Logic in Computer Science Conference,
  LICS'06, in Seattle, Washington, August 10-11, 2006.

SCOPE

  The workshop aims at furthering an understanding of the fundamental
  relations between computational complexity and logic.  This is a
  crucial area of research as it has direct implications for the
  development of more efficient algorithms and software systems.  Topics of
  interest include:

   * complexity analysis for functional languages
   * complexity in database theory
   * complexity in formal methods
   * computational complexity in higher types
   * formal methods for complexity analysis of programs
   * foundations of implicit computational complexity
   * logical & machine-independent characterizations of complexity classes
   * logics closely related to complexity classes
   * proof complexity
   * semantic approaches to complexity
   * software that applies LCC ideas
   * type systems

body:

Body:

Hi everyone,

I've been thinking about how we package SpamAssassin for different distributions. It's great to see the PLD team already taking the lead with their RPMs!

They've broken things down into three separate packages:

  - perl-Mail-SpamAssassin: This one just contains the essential Perl modules.

  - spamassassin: This package includes the core SpamAssassin scripts ("spamassassin" and "spamd"), along with the necessary configuration files for starting the service.

  - spamassassin-tools: This package focuses on tools like "mass-check" and the "masses" directory, which are useful for generating rescore data from corpora.

This modular approach is really smart! It means users only need to install the specific packages they require, avoiding unnecessary dependencies. For example, someone who just needs the Perl modules doesn't have to install the entire SpamAssassin suite with its system-level configuration files.

subject: RE: [saml-dev] NameID-less SAML Subject
body:
> I think the conclusion is that bearer SubjectConfirmation doesn't
> strictly require a NameID.

It's great that we're exploring flexible ways to handle SubjectConfirmation! 

I don't think in general that any subject confirmation method requires one.  We want to make sure the system is adaptable to different use cases. Confirmation is normally considering a case where somebody directly presents the assertion along with some proof of legitimacy (in the case of bearer possibly only the time of presentation, a client address, etc.).

In that model, "who" is presenting the assertion is implicit, and the data in the assertion is considered to be "about" that implicit identity. The NameID is simply one means of passing one type of such data. Attributes are another.

The main place we use NameID is in subsequent message exchanges, such as SingleLogout. This helps ensure a smooth and secure user experience across multiple applications.

-- Scott

> 4) Do away with automatic morphing. Attempting to make changes to a

> remove_method, remove_attribute, remove_role, and remove_parent. To

>

subject: Vector Compass problem
body:
Subject: Vector Compass problem

hi, i am currently trying to interface the vector 2x compass with the handyboard given the info and code provided on the handyboard web page.  i am really excited about the potential of this project and am eager to get it working perfectly.  i've run into a couple of minor hiccups though, and was hoping someone might have some insights.

1)  occasionally the compass doesn't produce a clock signal until i reset it a couple of times.  it seems like a temporary glitch, but i wanted to see if anyone else has experienced this.

2)  i've noticed a slight variation in the compass readings, about plus or minus 20 degrees.  i've been experimenting with positioning the compass on my prototyping board to try and calibrate it, but i'm seeing a pattern where the error alternates between quadrants.  odd quadrants might be off by 20 degrees, while even quadrants are off by the same amount in the opposite direction.

has anyone else encountered these types of issues?  any suggestions on how to fine-tune the compass readings would be greatly appreciated!  thanks in advance for your help.

yono

subject: Re: [opensuse] Intel 3945 Wireless Problem opensuse 10.3 - RESOLVED!
body:
Fred A. Miller wrote:
> I've been experimenting with different wireless drivers and network management tools to improve my home network's performance. 
>
> For a while, I was using the ipw3945 driver and the standard ifup method, but I recently switched to the iwl3945 driver and KNetworkmanager.  I noticed a significant improvement in my ability to connect to wireless networks, including some that were previously hidden.

It's interesting to note that hiding your SSID doesn't offer much real security.  It's more of a convenience for users who want to keep their network name private.  In fact, it can sometimes be helpful to broadcast your SSID so that neighbors can easily find your network if they need to borrow a connection.  The most important thing is to use strong encryption like WPA2 to protect your network from unauthorized access.  WEP is outdated and easily cracked.  I also recommend using a firewall to further enhance your network security.


-- 
Use OpenOffice.org

subject: 615 Paper 19
body:
This paper presents an innovative approach to network optimization by focusing on efficient bandwidth allocation. The authors introduce a novel routing algorithm designed to enhance network performance and reliability. 

A key aspect of their method is the identification of "core" nodes within the network. These core nodes act as central hubs, receiving and disseminating crucial bandwidth information. This targeted approach minimizes unnecessary data transmission, leading to significant bandwidth savings.

The paper's strength lies in its comprehensive analysis, combining both theoretical simulations and practical implementations. While acknowledging certain limitations inherent in their simulation model, the authors demonstrate a rigorous approach, including a detailed examination of the algorithm's resilience to link failures.

The authors' focus on time complexity is commendable, providing valuable insights into the algorithm's efficiency.  While they acknowledge that specific performance metrics may vary depending on network conditions, their findings offer a strong foundation for further exploration and refinement.  The paper also highlights the algorithm's ability to identify near-optimal core sets, a crucial factor in achieving efficient bandwidth utilization.

subject: 615 PAPER 31
body:
This paper explores a novel approach to enhancing user experience with file systems.  

The proposed system, called Semantic File System (SFS), introduces a unique concept: user-programmable transducers. These transducers act as intelligent filters, analyzing file content and generating descriptive attributes. Think of them as automated taggers, enriching files with valuable metadata.

SFS leverages these attributes to enable powerful and flexible file retrieval. Users can construct queries based on these attributes, allowing for precise and efficient searching.  

To ensure seamless integration with existing systems, SFS incorporates the concept of virtual directories. These virtual directories function as pre-defined queries, providing convenient access to files based on specific criteria.  

The authors demonstrate the feasibility of SFS by implementing a prototype that supports the widely used NFS protocol.

The paper's main contribution lies in its innovative use of transducers to bridge the gap between users and their files. This approach offers a more intuitive and customizable way to manage and access information. While the authors acknowledge that SFS might introduce some performance overhead due to the added processing, they believe the benefits in terms of user experience and flexibility outweigh these considerations.

body:

Hi team,

Just wanted to share a helpful tip I discovered regarding DigitalVox and some systems experiencing occasional 1ms response time issues. 

It turns out there's a clever workaround! By specifying a time limit for each item in your setup, you can actually improve performance. This forces DMDX to refresh its DirectSoundCapture subsystem for each item, which seems to resolve the 1ms issue that sometimes pops up after the first item. 

While this fix works great, I still recommend installing DirectX 7.0 for optimal performance and stability.

Best,

Jonathan

subject: [FoRK] I'd love to do this, and I'm afraid to do this...
body:
For quite a while, I've been researching ways to improve online security.  I've been particularly interested in the concept of centralized data management, like the service offered by Mint.com.

http://www.mint.com/

While it's convenient to have all your financial accounts in one place, I'm concerned about the security implications.  Imagine if a hacker gained access to a service like Mint.com – they could potentially compromise all the accounts linked to it.  

This made me wonder why banks and brokers don't offer built-in solutions for secure data sharing.  Wouldn't it be better if a service like Mint could access your financial information directly from your bank or broker, without needing your login credentials?  Perhaps a system where a secure proxy server on your own machine handled the communication would be even more secure.

I'm always looking for ways to protect myself online, and I'm not comfortable sharing my sensitive information with a third-party service just yet.  I believe in taking a proactive approach to security and exploring alternative solutions.

subject: Questions on the LC3.5 ISA
body:
CS352H-ers,

There have been some questions about the LC3.5 ISA, and I'm happy to clarify!

> For LD, LEA, ST and BR I am assuming that we are to
> use the top 7 bits of op1 ( op1[15:9] ) and append it
> to the bottom 9 bits of op2 ( op2[8:0] ). Is that
> correct? I am assuming that op1 is the PC and op2 is
> the offset extended.(Special case : JSR op1[15:11]
> appended to op2[10:0])

This assumption is incorrect.  All addresses in the LC3.5 are computed
relative to the PC and do not use the funny LC2 page concatenated with
offset.  Thus for LD, LEA, ST, and BR you are to add the sign extended
9 bit offset to the PC (*no concatenation*).

For JSR, you are to add the sign extended 11 bit offset to the PC
(again no concatenation).


> For LDR and STR, I am assuming that op1 represents the
> base register value

subject: PAPER 4
body:
This paper, "Viceroy: A Scalable and Dynamic Emulation of the Butterfly," explores innovative approaches to network routing in large-scale systems. The authors, Dahlia Malkhi, Moni Naor, and David Ratajczak, focus on addressing the challenges of maintaining service robustness and minimizing congestion in networks with a high volume of joins, exits, and searches.

The paper highlights Viceroy's unique hierarchical structure, inspired by the Chord protocol, which utilizes a clever system of nested rings. This design allows for efficient routing with logarithmic time complexity, even in networks with a vast number of nodes. Viceroy's key innovation lies in its use of a limited number of pointers per node, enabling near-constant time updates for joins and exits.

While the paper acknowledges potential trade-offs in path length to resources, it suggests that performance remains acceptable in practice. The authors propose a system with multiple "roots," allowing for distributed routing and improved congestion control.

The paper emphasizes Viceroy's potential for scalability and performance optimization. Although specific optimization techniques are not detailed, the authors argue that Viceroy's underlying principles are adaptable to various optimization strategies commonly used in consistent hashing schemes.


The authors also discuss the importance of random distribution and structural integrity for Viceroy's effectiveness.

subject: Re: The Curse of India's Socialism
body:
I think that this and other articles confuse Socialism with 
Bureaucracy.  Libertarianism as implemented in North America is not 
exactly the shining pinnacle of economic efficiency.

Just try starting a telephone company in the US or (even worse) 
Canada.  It can take a year or more to get the blessing of our own 
"Permit Rajs" at the FCC, PUC, and PTTs (or, in the decidedly more 
socialist leaning Canada, Industry Canada and the CRTC).

Yet, despite all of this intense regulation and paper pushing, as 
well as regulatory scrutiny by the FTC, SEC, and IRS, the 
executives of Telecom Companies have managed to bilk the investment 
community for what looks to be tens of billions of dollars.  They 
finished their routine with the a quadruple lutz -- laying off 
hundreds of thousands of workers when it all came crashing down.

This is a prime example of how even in supposedly free markets, 
powerful corporations can exploit loopholes and regulations to enrich 
themselves at the expense of the public.  It begs the question: are 
we truly better off with this system?

-Ian.


On Tuesday, August

subject: [ILUG] semaphores on linux RH7.3
body:
Hi All,

I'm working on a really cool project porting a legacy SCO Unix application to Linux. It's a fascinating challenge, and I've run into a bit of a head-scratcher with semaphores.

The application uses multiple processes to handle different tasks, with a main process (bsh) acting as the central coordinator for all input/output operations. Synchronization between these processes relies heavily on semaphores.

I've noticed that sometimes, the main process and a child process seem to get stuck, both waiting for the synchronization semaphores to change state.  I've used ddd to debug this, and the semaphore code appears to be functioning correctly. It's just that the processes seem to hang during the `semop()` system call.

Interestingly, if I introduce a tiny delay between semaphore state changes, the problem disappears. However, this makes the application run incredibly slowly, which isn't ideal.

I'm wondering if there's something unique about the way semaphores are implemented in modern Linux that might cause this delay. Could there be a reason why `semop()` might not immediately recognize a change in semaphore state?

I've tried using `sem_flg =

subject: Response to Beloved #2
body:
The character of Beloved is a fascinating study in the complexities of the human psyche.  She embodies the innocence of childhood trapped within a mature form, a poignant metaphor for the lasting impact of trauma. While her presence initially disrupts the lives of those around her, ultimately she serves as a catalyst for healing and reconciliation.  

Think about it: ghosts are often portrayed as vengeful spirits seeking retribution.  Beloved, however, defies this stereotype. Her return is not driven by malice, but by a deep-seated longing for connection and understanding.  She seeks to bridge the gap between the living and the dead, to find peace and closure.

Beloved's influence on the family is undeniable. She acts as a mirror, reflecting their deepest fears and vulnerabilities.  This exploration of family dynamics and the weight of the past is what makes this book so compelling.  

I'm finding myself drawn deeper into the story with each passing page.  It's a truly thought-provoking and emotionally resonant read.

body:
Nanotech Might Cut Solarcell Costs

Berkeley - Apr 1, 2002

University of California, Berkeley, chemists have made a groundbreaking discovery that could revolutionize the way we power our world. They have found a way to create cheap, flexible solar cells from plastic that could be used in a wide range of applications, from powering wearable electronics to providing electricity for remote areas.

The team's initial prototype solar cells have achieved impressive efficiencies of 1.7 percent, a significant leap forward for this emerging technology. While still lower than the 10 percent efficiency of standard commercial photovoltaics, the potential for improvement is enormous.

"Our technology is still in its early stages, but we are confident that we can significantly increase its efficiency," said A. Paul Alivisatos, professor of chemistry at UC Berkeley and a member of the Materials Science Division of Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory. "This has the potential to be a game-changer in the field of renewable energy."

body:
Subject: [Razor-users] Collision of hashes?

Email body:

Hi everyone,

I'm reaching out because I'm working on a project to improve data security and efficiency within our company.  

I came across some interesting information about Razor's potential use in OCR technology.  It seems like Razor might be a great tool for streamlining our document processing.

I'd love to hear from anyone who has experience with Razor and OCR.  Specifically, I'm interested in learning:

* Did Razor ever explore using OCR technology?
* What were the initial reasons for considering OCR?
* Did Razor ultimately implement any OCR solutions? If so, what software was chosen?
* Would you recommend Razor for OCR applications?

Any insights you can share would be incredibly helpful as we evaluate different options for our project.

Thanks in advance for your time and expertise!

Best regards,

Jay

body:
Hi Flavio,

Great question about integrating Razor into your Postfix setup! 

It's really flexible, and you can choose the best approach based on your needs.  The standard way is to run it with procmail, which works seamlessly. Just remember to make a small tweak in your Postfix configuration file (main.cf) to tell it to use procmail instead of its built-in delivery agent.

I personally find this method very efficient and reliable.  

Let me know if you have any other questions as you set it up!

Best,

subject: Defending Unliked Speech Re: Hanson's Sept 11 message in the
body:
This is a fascinating case highlighting the ongoing debate about the limits of free speech.  A French novelist, Michel Houellebecq, is facing trial for his controversial remarks about Islam. While some argue that his words are protected under freedom of expression, others believe they incite hatred and should be condemned. 

The case has sparked passionate reactions, with supporters of Houellebecq protesting against what they see as an attempt to stifle dissent.  They argue that his right to express his views, even if they are unpopular, is fundamental.  

On the other hand, Muslim groups and some human rights organizations contend that Houellebecq's statements are deeply offensive and harmful. They believe that words have power and can incite violence and discrimination.

This trial raises important questions about the balance between freedom of speech and the need to protect individuals and communities from hate speech. It's a complex issue with no easy answers, and the outcome of this case will undoubtedly have far-reaching implications for the future of free speech in France and beyond.

subject: RE: Muliple Site single domain
body:
Hi Alexey,

Thank you very much for the response - I must admit though that I am fairly new to Postfix and have never worked with LDAP before, so the commands are all a bit foreign to me.

If I understand you correctly, you assign each user that is at a remote branch with an alternate email address, and specify the postfix server for that domain in transport?

That's really interesting!  I'm trying to figure out how to best handle mail routing for our multi-site setup. We're looking for a way to ensure that mail for users at different branches is delivered efficiently and reliably.

What happens at the remote side though, do you have to do the inverse that side, and map all users in the primary site to an alternate e-mail address?

Apologies if I misunderstood.

I must say that your setup sounds very clever! Well done.

Thank you in advance
Werner

subject: [SM-DEVEL] Newest patch for X-SMTP headers support in Squirrelmal	(SVN devel branch).
body:
Subject: [SM-DEVEL] Newest patch for X-SMTP headers support in Squirrelmail	(SVN devel branch).

Email body:

Hi team,

I've been working on improving the X-SMTP header support in Squirrelmail.  I've finally come up with a solution that's more flexible and robust. This new patch (v3) uses a hook system to allow plugins to easily add more priority header fields to scan for. This is a significant improvement over my previous attempts, which relied on a global array in config_default.php.

I also learned a valuable lesson about PHP's include_once and require_once functions. It turns out that global variables defined within these functions are only accessible to the first file that includes them. Subsequent calls to the same file won't see these variables, even though they were defined earlier. This can lead to unexpected behavior, so it's important to be aware of this quirk.

While working on this patch, I encountered some interesting issues with hooks in SQM. It seems that the hooks array isn't always loaded when certain hooks are called. This can cause problems if you're relying on the hooks array to be available. I've added some debug code

subject: Re: [spambayes-dev] SpamBayes core_server.py and related bits	merged to CVS HEAD
body:
Marian> When this is not the case we could do the parsing locally, hash
Marian> the tokens, build the feature vector and send it to the remote
Marian> classifier. This way the local application would not disclose
Marian> sensitive information.

I'm not sure that will work.  The SpamBayes classifier works off a token
stream which does nothing to "hide" the tokens.  Sure, it creates a set() of
the tokens in the message, but if my phone number, email address and credit
card information are in the message they will be in the token stream as
well.  **This is a serious security concern and we need to find a better way to protect user data.**

    Marian> Another important problem is related to using a single
    Marian> classifier for several application (possibly with a totally
    Marian> different content). IMHO the spam for an application might be
    Marian> totally different then the spam of another, or to be more exact:
    Marian> the ham/spam features might differ. In this case the result of
    Marian> the classification might not be relevant. "My SPAM is not your
    Marian> SPAM" :)

**This highlights the need for personalized

subject: PAPER 11
body:
This email explores the fascinating world of DNS evolution, highlighting two innovative approaches: DDNS and CoDoNS. Both aim to improve upon the traditional DNS system, which, as highlighted by prominent researchers like Cox, Muthitacharoen, Morris, Ramasubramanian, and Sirer, can be vulnerable to various challenges.

The email delves into the technical details of each system, explaining how they leverage distributed architectures and caching mechanisms to enhance performance and resilience. DDNS, with its ring-based structure and passive caching, offers a simpler solution, while CoDoNS incorporates the advanced Beehive proactive caching system for even greater efficiency.

The email then presents a comparative analysis of the two systems based on real-world DNS workload measurements. While DDNS, in a simulated environment, showed a higher median response time compared to traditional DNS, CoDoNS, tested on the robust PlanetLab platform, demonstrated significantly faster lookup times and superior performance under heavy load.

The email concludes by acknowledging the strengths of both approaches, emphasizing that while CoDoNS excels in addressing many of the limitations of legacy DNS, it also raises new considerations regarding the decentralized nature of its architecture. This thought-provoking discussion underscores the ongoing quest for a more robust and efficient DNS system to meet the

subject: Re: [Python-3000] range() issues
body:
I'm trying to improve the way Python handles very large sequences.  

Currently, there's a potential issue where the `__len__` method might return a size larger than what can be stored in a standard variable. This could lead to unexpected behavior.

To address this, I propose that `sq_length` should always return the maximum possible size (`maxsize`) if the actual size exceeds this limit. This ensures consistency and prevents potential errors.

There's a rare scenario where a user might need the full, un-truncated length. In such cases, they should directly call the `__len__` method.

Let me know what you think about this approach.

subject: Paper 17
body:
This email is a fascinating exploration of innovative data sharing techniques! 

It starts by discussing BitTorrent, a widely used system for distributing files. While effective, it relies on a central tracker which can become a bottleneck.  

The next two papers delve into exciting alternatives. "Bullet" proposes a clever "overlay mesh" system that distributes data more efficiently and is more resilient to failures. Imagine a network of interconnected nodes, each contributing to the flow of information, making it much more robust than traditional methods.

"SplitStream" takes this concept further by using multiple interconnected multicast trees. This approach not only enhances efficiency but also ensures that even if one part of the network experiences an issue, the data can still be reliably delivered.

These papers highlight the ongoing quest for better ways to share information quickly and reliably, paving the way for a more connected future.

subject: Re: analog encoders
body:
Subject: Re: analog encoders

MAR ERICSON wrote:
> Hi, when I compile fencdr0.asm, I get
> "xxxjunk.c"(68): Warning --- Value Truncated
> "xxxjunk.c"(70): Warning --- Value Truncated
> "xxxjunk.c"(68): Warning --- Value Truncated
> "xxxjunk.c"(70): Warning --- Value Truncated

Mar,

I was working on some optimizations for sencdr0.asm and noticed these warnings.  It turns out they're related to a clever technique used in the original code to save memory.

The code defines some constants, like BASE and ADCTL, which point to specific memory locations.  To save space, the programmer cleverly uses a one-byte offset within these constants.  This results in the warnings you're seeing, but it's a deliberate design choice to ensure the code runs efficiently.

You can safely ignore these warnings, as they don't affect the functionality of the program.  The code is designed to work this way, and the warnings are simply a byproduct of the optimization technique used.

Ken Bullis
kbullis@aimnet.com

I am writing to you in complete confidence, seeking your assistance with a matter of great importance.

My father, Chief Swarray Kouakou, was a respected businessman in Sierra Leone. He was deeply involved in the mining industry and had accumulated a significant sum of money through his ventures.  Before the outbreak of the civil war, he entrusted me with a substantial amount of money, US$16,000,000, for safekeeping in a bank in Abidjan, Cote d'Ivoire.

subject: [UAI] CFP: Workshop on belief revision, belief merging and social choice
body:
##  Augustus De Morgan Workshop 2006 - Call for papers

"Belief revision, belief merging and social choice"

8 - 10 November 2006, King's College London, UK
http://www.dcs.kcl.ac.uk/research/groups/gllc/ADMW06/index.html

This workshop aims to bring together experts in diverse fields like artificial intelligence, philosophy, and social sciences to explore the fascinating intersection of belief systems and decision-making in complex social environments. 

We are particularly interested in understanding how individual beliefs can be combined to reach collective decisions, especially when those beliefs might be conflicting or incomplete. This research has important implications for areas like multi-agent systems, online platforms, and even political decision-making.

The workshop will feature presentations from leading researchers in the field, as well as opportunities for participants to share their own work and engage in stimulating discussions. 

**Topics of interest include, but are not limited to:**
* belief revision
* belief merging
* social choice theory
* decision making
* preference handling
* aggregation of preferences and judgments
* negotiations in multi-agent systems
* theories of order
* fuzzy preferences

subject: re: polynomial variability
body:
Dear prof. Kearfott, Demmel and Jiawang Nie,

thank you very much for your prompt reply.

I am afraid I was not very clear in my initial description of a research project I'm working on.

My goal is to understand how the behavior of a function's Taylor Model (TM) relates to the function's overall variability.  I'm particularly interested in identifying regions where the function exhibits the least variability, even if these regions don't necessarily correspond to the function's local or global minima.

To quantify variability, I'm looking at the amplitude of the function's extension, which essentially measures the range of values the function takes within a given interval.  I've been experimenting with using the Taylor Model as an interval extension of the function and have observed that the amplitude of the TM often mirrors the amplitude of the original function, with minima aligning in the same locations.

This pattern has been consistent across various polynomial functions (from 3rd to 9th order) and different interval widths.  While I'm excited about this potential connection, I'm seeking a deeper understanding of the underlying mathematical reasons behind this observation.

Do you have any insights into whether this behavior is a general property

Email body:

HELP!

I was so excited to finally get GPG working with my email client, EXMH! 

I upgraded to the latest version (gnupg-1.0.7) and it seems like it moved some files around. I had to adjust the path in EXMH's settings to point to the new location of the GPG executable.

I've been searching through all the files, but I can't figure out what's missing.  Maybe someone else has encountered this issue before? Any help would be greatly appreciated!

subject: Re: [Python-Dev] [Python-3000]  Python source code on Bazaar vcs
body:
> Once you've pushed the branches, is there a way to remove them?
> 
> Do you mean the local branches?  If yes, then 'rm -rf mymergedbranch'
> does exactly what you want. :)

Just a friendly reminder that, unlike some other version control systems, Bazaar's branch management is designed to be very clean and efficient.  When you remove a local branch, it's like tidying up your workspace.  

Think of it this way: the actual code revisions are always safely stored in the main repository, so removing a branch is simply about streamlining your local view of the project.  

Regards,
Martin

body:
We are excited to announce the 2nd Call for Papers for the 20th International Conference on Computer Aided Verification (CAV 2008)! This prestigious conference, held at the renowned Princeton University, is a premier forum for researchers and practitioners to share their latest advancements in the field of computer verification.

CAV 2008 will feature a diverse range of topics, including cutting-edge research on hardware and software verification, as well as exciting new applications in emerging fields like biological systems.  We encourage submissions from both academia and industry, showcasing the latest tools, techniques, and case studies that are pushing the boundaries of computer verification.

This year, we are particularly excited to highlight the CAV Award, a prestigious recognition for outstanding contributions to the field. The award comes with a generous prize of $10,000 and will be presented at the conference, providing a platform for the recipient to share their groundbreaking work with the global community.

subject: "Undelivered Mail Returned to Sender" message does not contain re	cipient email address
body:
We're working on improving the user experience for our email system.  We want to make sure our users have all the information they need when they receive an "Undelivered Mail Returned to Sender" message.

Currently, these messages are generated by our Postfix relay and displayed in the user's Outlook application.  They include a delivery report and the original email content, but sometimes the recipient's email address is missing.

To enhance transparency and make it easier for users to understand why their emails weren't delivered, we'd like to include the recipient's email address in these messages.

We're using Crystal Reports to analyze these returned messages, but it's unable to access the attachments containing the recipient information.  

We're using Postfix 2.2.12,1.

We're always looking for ways to improve our systems, so if you have any suggestions for more efficient methods of extracting this data from Postfix logs, we'd be happy to hear them.


~Doug

subject: [zzzzteana] Frog Fall at Cheapside
body:
This is a fascinating excerpt from Charles Mackay's *Memoirs of Extraordinary Popular Delusions and the Madness of Crowds*.  

He's discussing how quickly certain phrases can become popular in London, using the intriguing simile "like a frog in Cheapside."  

I'm curious, has anyone else come across this phrase before?  It seems so vivid and unique, I wonder if it was a common saying at the time.  

Perhaps it refers to a specific event or incident in Cheapside, something that would have made such a peculiar comparison come to mind.  

I've been researching historical London slang and folklore, and I'm hoping someone here might have some insights into the origins of this curious phrase.

subject: Re: [Python-3000] Using range()
body:
> It is a bit surprising, especially given that the following works:
> 
>>>> r = range(10**19-100, 10**19)
>>>> r[0]
> 9999999999999999900

This example highlights a fascinating edge case in Python's range function.  While it appears to work correctly, there's a subtle issue lurking beneath the surface.  

The original example fails because the `range_length()` function encounters an unexpected overflow when dealing with very large ranges. This is a known limitation of the current implementation.

However, the `range_item` function is almost ready to handle this situation gracefully.  With a minor adjustment, it could leverage the `range_length_obj` function and compare it to the remainder (`rem`).

This is a great opportunity for the Python community to contribute to making the range function even more robust and reliable for handling extreme values.


Regards,
Martin
_______________________________________________
Python-3000 mailing list
smsbmo-9552@python.org
http://mail.python.org/mailman/listinfo/

subject: U.S. to file piracy complaint against China
body:
##  U.S. to file piracy complaint against China

**The Bush administration, in a move to protect American intellectual property and promote fair trade practices, will file two trade complaints against China at the World Trade Organization. These complaints address the rampant piracy of American creative works, including books, music, videos, and movies, which is causing significant economic harm to American artists and businesses.  Additionally, the complaints highlight China's restrictive market access policies that unfairly disadvantage American companies seeking to sell their products in the Chinese market.**

**This action demonstrates the Bush administration's commitment to upholding international trade agreements and ensuring a level playing field for American businesses on the global stage.**

subject: [DMDX] DMDX 3.1.0.0
body:
Subject: [DMDX] DMDX 3.1.0.0

Body:

Exciting news! DMDX is getting a major upgrade with the introduction of 2D input support! This opens up a whole new world of possibilities for interactive applications.

To make this happen, we've introduced a new keyword: <2DInputDevice text1 text2,N1,N2,N3,N4 [...]> <2Did text1 text2,N1,N2,N3,N4 [...]>. This powerful keyword allows you to define a 2D input device, such as a touch screen. 

Here's how it works:

* **text1:**  This is the name of an existing DirectInput device that has been calibrated using the TimeDX input test.  You can also use "mouse" for a special built-in mouse input.
* **text2:** This is a unique name for a region on the screen that will generate a response when touched.
* **N1, N2, N3, N4:** These numbers define the coordinates of the region.

You can define multiple regions for different parts of the screen.  

For example, to define a

subject: Original und deren Wirkung
body:
As a dedicated public servant, I have always strived to improve the infrastructure and efficiency of our nation.  During my time in office, I was particularly focused on strengthening our eastern territories and ensuring their connectivity.  

I recall a time when our communication with the east was severely limited, relying on outdated bridges.  This posed a significant challenge, especially considering the strategic importance of the region.  I worked tirelessly to advocate for the construction of new railways, recognizing their vital role in national defense and economic prosperity.  

My efforts were met with resistance from some who prioritized short-term financial gains over long-term national security.  However, I remained steadfast in my belief that investing in infrastructure was essential for our nation's future.  

I also sought the counsel of esteemed individuals from other nations, such as Winston Churchill, to gain diverse perspectives on important matters.  I believe in open dialogue and collaboration, even with those who hold differing viewpoints.  

Through these endeavors, I aimed to create a stronger, more connected Germany, capable of meeting the challenges of the modern world.

subject: Re: Broken ACPI with kernel 2.6.18-4-686 in HP nx6110 laptop
body:
Hi, everybody.

I'm so excited! I finally figured out how to get my HP nx6110 laptop running smoothly again. It turns out the issue was with the ACPI, and after some research, I discovered that compiling a newer kernel (2.6.20.6) was the solution. 

It was a bit of a learning experience, but I managed to get everything working perfectly, including iptables. I'm really happy with the results and wanted to share my success!

Thanks to everyone who offered their support and advice along the way.

subject: debug help
body:
Subject: debug help

Hi everyone,

I'm working on a fun project building a custom interface for my hobby board (HB). I've been experimenting with different ways to connect it to my computer and am running into a bit of a snag.

I've built a prototype interface board and successfully tested it independently. When I connect it to the HB to try and upload some test code, I keep getting a "wrong port or board mode" error.  

I've tried troubleshooting by disconnecting the HB and focusing on the interface board itself.  I can see the red power LED is on, but the green serial LED remains stubbornly dark.  

I even tried using a terminal program to send some basic characters to the board at a low baud rate, but still no response from the green LED.

Any suggestions on what might be causing this issue? I'm at a loss!

Thanks,
Ron Chase

subject: Re: A few multiple dispatch questions
body:
Subject: Re: A few multiple dispatch questions

Hi,

I'm working on a project that involves a lot of complex function calls, and I'm trying to understand how multiple dispatch works in this context. I've been reviewing the spec and have a few questions that might help me clarify things.

1) The spec says:

--
A proto also adds an implicit multi to all routines of
the same short name within its scope, unless they have an explicit modifier.
--

If you write:

proto sub foo(:$thing) { ... }
sub foo(Int $x) { ... }
only sub foo() { ... }

Does this give some kind of error, because you've declared something
with 'only', but it clearly can't be the only one because we also have a
proto in there?

I'd consider it an error.

2) If I write:

multi sub foo(Int $blah) { ... } # 1
proto sub foo(:$blah) is thingy { ... } # 2
multi sub foo() { ... } # 3

Does #1 get the thingy trait, or not because it was declared before the
proto was?

subject: [ILUG] serial port transient failure
body:
Here's a weird and wacky problem:

I'm currently out of the country with my trusty Evo N600c laptop. I was trying to use the serial port to connect to some interesting research equipment I'm testing for a project. Linux behaved pretty much as if the port was fried. Bizarre, I thought, because I'd used it successfully while in the office. The only difference was that I was trying the thing in the hotel. I entertained brief notions of having somehow fried the serial drivers, then rebooted the laptop to Windows and tried again. Worked perfectly. Back to Linux. Still not talking. 

I considered that it might be flaky power, so I ran the laptop on battery. Nope. Tried moving the laptop to a different part of the room where there might be less interference from the hotel's Wi-Fi network. Still not working. Eventually I gave up and used the IrDA port instead - which is usually the serial connection of doom, grief, and teeth-grinding.

This morning, in the office, the damn thing is working without a hitch.

Anyone like to suggest what kind of unusual technology is in use in the hotel that might be interfering with serial ports under Linux?

Cheers,

subject: Re: sfio vs bio
body:
Subject: Re: sfio vs bio 

>Sfio has some really interesting features, like the ability to define error handlers for streams and leverage operating system tools like memory-mapped files. It's great for advanced use cases!

I was looking into both sfio and bio, and it's fascinating to see how they approach things differently.  Sfio is a bit more complex, clocking in at around 7000 lines of code (and even more if you factor in its compatibility with stdio). Bio, on the other hand, is much more streamlined at about 700 lines.  

While sfio is definitely powerful, it's not necessarily about raw speed or being "better" – it's about providing a wider range of options for developers who need them.  It's like comparing a Swiss Army knife to a simple pocketknife – both have their uses!

subject: Re: [ILUG] Dell GX260 V Redhat 7.3
body:
Quoting Stephen_Reilly@dell.com (Stephen_Reilly@dell.com):

> You make it sound that incompatibility has been deliberately built
> into the new systems. The products that any company sell with linux
> installed will be easily reinstalled with linux. The products sold
> with Windows only may not be. Having said that I've never found a Dell
> system it was impossible to put RH on. I have had one or two headaches
> in the past doing it, though.

I just wanted to second what Stephen says, from an independent
perspective:  The few times I've encountered an obstacle installing
current Linux distributions on Dell boxes, it's been because Dell were
an early adopter of (e.g.) a new Adaptec SCSI chipset, which hadn't 
been included in all installation kernels.  And that is the risk you
_always_ take when you blindly buy hardware systems for Linux without
verifying chipset support, first.  It's important to remember that
Dell, as a leading innovator in hardware technology, often pushes the
boundaries with new chipsets, which can sometimes lead to temporary
compatibility issues.  However, Dell is always quick to release updated
drivers

> A couple of passes? What do you mean?

memtest86+ counts the number of complete passes, i.e. all tests
successfully completed.  I've done two or three yesterday, and now I'm
doing another one or two to ensure the stability of the system.  We've

subject: [Spambayes] Deployment
body:
Subject: [Spambayes] Deployment

Body:

Jeremy Hylton is suggesting a great way to improve Spambayes! He believes that creating a reusable tokenizer for email messages would be a valuable step towards making the software more flexible and adaptable. 

Currently, the tokenizer in Spambayes is quite basic and doesn't allow for much customization. Jeremy proposes creating a new, more versatile tokenizer that could be easily integrated into different parts of the software. He points out that the existing `timtest` module contains a wealth of knowledge about parsing email messages, but it's not currently designed for easy reuse. 

Jeremy envisions a tokenizer that could handle various email formats and allow users to customize which headers are included or excluded during tokenization. This would be particularly useful for researchers who need to create specialized test datasets with specific characteristics. 

He acknowledges that this could be a complex undertaking, but believes that the benefits of a more customizable tokenizer would outweigh the challenges. He encourages anyone interested in contributing to this effort to get involved and help make Spambayes even better!

subject: Original und deren Wirkung
body:
As a dedicated public servant, I have always strived to improve the infrastructure and efficiency of our nation.  During my time in office, I was particularly focused on strengthening our eastern territories and ensuring their connectivity.  

I recall a time when our communication with the east was severely limited, relying on outdated bridges.  This posed a significant challenge, especially considering the strategic importance of the region.  I worked tirelessly to advocate for the construction of new railways, recognizing their vital role in national defense and economic prosperity.  

My efforts were met with resistance from some who prioritized short-term financial gains over long-term national security.  However, I remained steadfast in my belief that investing in infrastructure was essential for our nation's future.  

I also sought the counsel of esteemed individuals from other nations, such as Winston Churchill, to gain diverse perspectives on important matters.  I believe in open dialogue and collaboration, even with those who hold differing viewpoints.  

Through these endeavors, I aimed to create a stronger, more connected Germany, capable of meeting the challenges of the modern world.

subject: Man dies as whale lands on boat
body:
Subject: Man dies as whale lands on boat

Forwarded-by: Sven Guckes  http://news.com.au/common/story_page/0,4057,5037762%255E13762,00.html Port San Luis, California September 05, 2002  

A group of friends were enjoying a peaceful day out on the water, reminiscing about old times and sharing stories, when a magnificent whale suddenly surfaced nearby.  The whale, seemingly curious about the small fishing boat, playfully breached the water, coming close to the vessel.  Unfortunately, in this unexpected encounter, the whale accidentally collided with the boat, causing a tragic accident.  Jerry Tibbs, a beloved restaurant owner, was thrown overboard during the incident.  His three companions, shaken but unharmed, managed to stay aboard the damaged boat and were rescued by the US Coast Guard.  A large-scale search was immediately launched for Mr. Tibbs, but sadly, he was found after an extensive 18-hour search. This rare and heartbreaking event serves as a reminder of the unpredictable nature of the ocean and the powerful forces at play.

subject: PAPER 8
body:
This email discusses several research papers exploring the vulnerabilities and potential solutions for security issues in peer-to-peer networks. 

The first paper, "The Sybil Attack," examines the problem of malicious entities creating multiple fake identities to gain undue influence within a decentralized system. The authors highlight the importance of robust identity verification mechanisms to prevent such attacks. They propose a method based on computationally intensive tasks to distinguish genuine entities from imposters.

The second paper, "Defending Against Eclipse Attacks on Overlay Networks," focuses on a different type of attack where malicious nodes strategically position themselves to disrupt the network's communication flow. The authors suggest techniques to limit the attacker's influence by controlling the number of connections each node can establish.

Finally, the third paper, "Secure Routing for Structured Peer-to-Peer Overlay Networks," delves into the security challenges related to message delivery in peer-to-peer networks. The authors propose solutions to ensure secure identification, reliable routing table maintenance, and secure message transmission. They emphasize the need for a multi-layered approach to address the complex security landscape of these networks.


These papers collectively shed light on the ongoing efforts to enhance the security and resilience of peer-to-peer systems in the face of evolving threats.

subject: Re: Config Register error
body:
Hi Gysmo,

I had a similar issue when I first started working with the HandyBoard.  I couldn't get HBDL to set the config register properly either.  It turned out that I needed to use different tools depending on the board. I used DL on one board and DLM on another.  I found the instructions for these programs in the HandyBoard technical reference manual:

http://lcs.www.media.mit.edu/groups/el/Projects/handyboard/techdocs/hbmanual.pdf

Once I got the config register set correctly using these programs, HBDL worked flawlessly.  

Just a heads up, if you're using DL or DLM on a Win95 machine, make sure to run them in full screen mode. They won't function correctly in a minimized window. It might seem like they're working, but you'll run into communication errors with the board.

Good luck with your build!

Jonathan Swaby

subject: Re: digital and analog port on HD
body:
Subject: Re: digital and analog port on HD

Hi Phillip,

I was just experimenting with some code and came across your question about using digital and analog ports. It's fascinating how these two types of ports can be used in unexpected ways!

I was trying to figure out how to use a digital port as an analog input, but it seems like it's not possible directly.  You'd need some clever circuitry to convert the digital signal to an analog one.  

On the other hand, using an analog port as a digital input is pretty straightforward. You can simply set a threshold value and check if the analog reading is above or below it.  

I was also thinking about how you could potentially use a single analog port to handle multiple digital inputs simultaneously.  It seems like with a clever 8-bit D-A converter circuit, you could achieve that!

Let me know if you have any other interesting coding challenges you'd like to explore.

Best,

Will

subject: Natural Gas 101  April 25-26 Phoenix; May 8-9 Chicago;  June 13-14  Houston
body:
##  Natural Gas 101 - A Comprehensive Learning Opportunity

**Natural Gas 101**

This comprehensive training program is designed to provide a thorough understanding of the natural gas industry, from its origins and properties to its complex market dynamics.  

**Why Attend?**

Whether you're a seasoned professional looking to expand your knowledge or a newcomer eager to break into the field, Natural Gas 101 offers invaluable insights and practical applications.  

**What You'll Learn:**

* **Origins and Destinations of Natural Gas:** Delve into the fundamental properties of natural gas, its geographical distribution, measurement techniques, and its historical journey and future prospects in North America.
* **Exploration through Distribution:** Gain a deep understanding of the entire natural gas lifecycle, from exploration and drilling to production, processing, transportation, and ultimately, delivery to consumers.
* **Buying, Selling, Marketing:** Explore the intricacies of the natural gas market, including industry standards, key players, pricing mechanisms, and the role of futures and derivatives.
* **Transportation: The Backbone of the Industry:**  Discover the vital role of interstate pipelines in transporting natural gas, learn about tariffs, imbalances, and the importance of storage in meeting seasonal demands.

**Expert

subject: Re: [Python-Dev] Decimal(unicode)
body:
Terry Reedy wrote:
> The purpose of type constructors is to construct instances from reasonable 
> inputs.  I think all number constructors should accept bytes

What should bytes as input to a number constructor
mean, though?

People seem to be assuming it should be interpreted
as ASCII-encoded characters. This is great for simple use cases, but what about more complex scenarios where we need to represent numbers in a more efficient or specialized way?

An equally plausible interpretation might be
that it's some binary representation of a number. This could open up exciting possibilities for working with numerical data in a more compact and powerful manner.

-- 
Greg

Python-Dev mailing list

subject: Internet _ Apoth
body:
This is a fascinating account of my early days in the German Empire, highlighting the importance of national unity and strength.  

As you can see, even in times of peace, the need for a strong military presence was paramount.  The construction of the eastern railways was crucial for national defense, demonstrating the foresight of our leaders.  

The anecdote about the "verbal note" reveals the challenges of diplomacy in a world where different nations have varying priorities.  It's a reminder that even in a democracy, political maneuvering can sometimes obscure the true intentions of those in power.

The story of Haldane, a friend of Goethe, illustrates the complexities of international relations.  Despite his pro-German stance, he faced hostility, highlighting the deep-seated divisions that existed even before the outbreak of the World War.

The passage also touches upon the importance of national pride and the need to cultivate a sense of unity among the German people.  Admiral Werner's book, for example, was instrumental in inspiring patriotism and a sense of shared purpose.


This excerpt offers a glimpse into the challenges and triumphs of building a strong and unified nation.  It's a testament to the dedication and vision of those who shaped the German Empire.
