subject: Re: Battery Status
body:
Subject: Re: Battery Status

Hi Andre,

It's great to hear you're working on your robot project!  I remember when I first started out, electronics seemed so complicated.

To answer your questions:

1) You're right, voltage is a good indicator of how much charge is left in a battery. As the battery discharges, the voltage will naturally decrease.

2)  You can definitely use one of the analog ports on the Handy Board to measure the voltage. A voltage divider circuit is a simple way to do this. Just remember that nickel-cadmium batteries (nicads) tend to have a pretty sudden drop in voltage as they run out of power, so you might need to experiment a bit to find the best cutoff point for your calculations.

Good luck with your project! Let me know if you have any other questions.

Best,

Duncan

subject: Re: Please help a newbie compile mplayer :-)
body:
Subject: Re: Please help a newbie compile mplayer :-)

Email body:

Hi Rob,

I saw your post about compiling mplayer and thought I could offer some assistance.  I've had a lot of experience building software from source, especially for Red Hat systems. 

It sounds like you might be running into a common issue with source RPMs. Sometimes, if you've already installed a binary package, the source RPM might not be recognized correctly.  

To check, head over to /usr/src/redhat/SPECS/ and look for a file named "mplayer.spec". If you find it, you can use the command "rpm -bb mplayer.spec" to build a new binary package. This will create a fresh build in /usr/src/redhat/RPMS/i386/.

Let me know if that helps!

Best,

Matthias

subject: Re: [OT] Bogus MX opinions
body:
Richard Frovarp wrote:
>>
> We do something like nolisting. You will lose legit mail no matter 
> which trick you use. So it's best if you have a method of fixing that. 
> Our first mx record is a real smtp server, it's just firewalled off to 
> most of the world. It's used as a fast lane for our internal networks 
> so they aren't slowed down by spam attacks. If we run into a legit 
> server having issues (and there will be, don't let anyone else fool 
> you into thinking there won't be), we can just open up the firewall to 
> their SMTP and problem is solved.

I'm exploring different approaches to email delivery and wanted to share my thoughts.  While Richard's method of using a firewalled primary MX record seems interesting, I'm concerned about the potential for legitimate emails to be blocked.  

My experience suggests that relying solely on MX retry strategies can be unreliable, as spammers can easily adapt their techniques.  It's important to have robust safeguards in place to ensure that all legitimate emails are delivered successfully.

subject: ANN: mixed 0.2
body:
Subject: ANN: mixed 0.2

Body:

Hi everyone,

I'm excited to announce the release of 'mixed', a new module designed to make working with mixed numbers and fractions in Python a breeze! 

This module is perfect for anyone who needs to perform precise calculations involving both whole numbers and fractions. It can parse strings and floats, ensuring accurate representation and handling of arithmetic operations.

Here's a quick example of what 'mixed' can do:

```python
>>> from mixed import Mixed
>>> m1 = Mixed('4 2/3')
>>> m2 = Mixed('4/10')
>>> m2.reduced()
Mixed('2/5')
>>> m1 + m2
Mixed('5 1/15')
>>> print m1 / m2
11 2/3
>>> Mixed(-1.5)
Mixed('-1 1/2')
>>> Mixed(2./3.)
Mixed('2/3')
```

You can easily install 'mixed' using easy_install:

```
easy_install mixed
```

For those interested in contributing or exploring the development version, here are the links:

subject: itools 0.20.5 released
body:
Subject: itools 0.20.5 released

itools is a Python library designed to simplify the development and deployment of complex applications. It bundles a collection of specialized packages into a single, convenient meta-package.

This release introduces a groundbreaking new package, itools.abnf, which implements the Augmented Backus-Naur Form (RFC 4234) using a cutting-edge Scannerless Generalized-LR algorithm. This innovative approach promises significant performance improvements for parsing complex data structures. While still in its early stages, itools.abnf represents a major step forward in parsing technology.

We've also made significant enhancements to the XML parser, including the ability to process open files directly. This greatly simplifies the integration of itools with existing workflows.

To ensure optimal performance, itools now relies on the GLib library (version 2.14 or later). This powerful library provides a robust foundation for our core functionalities.

Numerous other improvements have been implemented, including enhanced support for CJK text strings in the catalog.

We encourage you to explore the full details of these exciting new features and bug fixes in the release notes:

[1] http://bugs.ikaaro.org/show_bug.cgi?

body:
(This appeared on the newsgroup, but didn't make it to the mailing list.)

From: jm@pasteur.FR (Jean Mehat)
Subject: (bad) surprise with the C compiler
Summary: non-atomic operation on structures
Date: Sat, 22 Mar 1997 17:29:48 GMT
Organization: Institut Pasteur, Paris

I was working on a fascinating new algorithm for optimizing graphics rendering in 3D environments.  During testing, I encountered a peculiar behavior with the C compiler that I wanted to share with the community.  

It seems that the following line of code:

	delta = Pt(-delta.y, delta.x)

expanded in a way I hadn't anticipated:

	delta = (Point){-delta.y, delta.x}

This unexpected expansion resulted in unintended consequences for my algorithm, as it essentially performed a series of individual assignments instead of a single, atomic operation.  

I'm eager to hear if anyone else has encountered this issue or has suggestions for workarounds.


--
Jean Mehat, universite de Paris 8 Vincennes a Saint Denis,
jm@univ-paris8.fr,

subject: Re: [Python-Dev] [Python-3000] Code freeze?
body:
Christian Heimes wrote:
> Is this documented somewhere? The docs say "See the
> :meth:`__getattribute__` method below for a way to actually get total
> control over attribute access.".

I've been looking into some interesting ways to improve Python's flexibility and wanted to share my findings.  It turns out there's a hidden mechanism for controlling attribute access using the `__getattribute__` method.

This method, while documented, isn't widely known and can be quite powerful.  I've discovered that defining it on a new-style class allows for very fine-grained control over how attributes are accessed.  

Interestingly, defining it on a new-style instance can also have unexpected effects, though it's not always reliable.  

While exploring this, I noticed a quirk in the new tempfile tests. They were working fine for me because I was testing on the trunk, where _TemporaryFileWrapper is a classic class. Classic classes handle special method lookup differently, so the tests worked as expected.

However, when I switched to a new-style class, the tests started failing. This is because proxies like _TemporaryFileWrapper that rely on `__getattr__` to provide special methods

body:

Brandon Allbery wrote: >>  >> I think the problem with general use of backtracking is that, while >> convenient, it's less efficient than improved algorithms are for any given >> task. subject: Bug in rc
body:
Subject: Bug in rc

Hi everyone,

I'm working on a fun little script to demonstrate a quirk I found in the `rc` interpreter. 

Take a look at this:

#!/bin/rc
for (iter in 1 2 3 4 5) {
	echo $iter
	sleep 5 &
	wait
}

It seems like the `wait` command is having a bit of trouble with the `for` loop's argument list.  I'm trying to figure out why it's behaving this way.  Maybe someone else has encountered this before?

Let me know if you have any insights!

Best,

Amos

subject: [perl #46543] Strange behavior of map inside @{}
body:
This is a bug report for perl from qxqoyvf-d7e@egg.flyinganvil.org,
generated with the help of perlbug 1.35 running under perl v5.8.8.


-----------------------------------------------------------------
I'm trying to understand how perl handles array manipulation within a map function. I've created a minimal script to illustrate a peculiar behavior I've encountered.

  sub r { map [$_], 4; }
  sub p { @{ shift; } }
  print   'r = ', r(),
       ', @r = ', @{ r(); },
      ', p() = ', p( r() ),
  $/;


  print  "@{  +map [1, 2, 3, $_], (4); }" # prints nothing
  print "@${ \\+map [1, 2, 3, $_], (4); }" # prints '1 2 3 4'


I've tested this against the latest bleed version (blead@31847) and observed consistent behavior across different perl versions.

subject: Re: [Python-3000] Python source code on Bazaar vcs
body:
Subject: Re: [Python-3000] Python source code on Bazaar vcs

Barry Warsaw schrieb:
> I'm happy to announce that we now have available for public  
> consumption, the Python source code for 2.5, 2.6 and 3.0 available  
> under the Bazaar distributed version control system.

This is fantastic news!  It's great to see the Python community embracing new technologies like Bazaar for source code management.  

Ubuntu users who want to get involved in the development of Python will need to update their bzr package:

sudo vi /etc/apt/sources.list.d/bzr.list
--- add ---
deb http://ppa.launchpad.net/bzr/ubuntu gutsy main
deb-src http://ppa.launchpad.net/bzr/ubuntu gutsy main
--- eof ---

sudo apt-get update

#  --force-yes because the packages aren't signed yet
sudo apt-get --force-yes -y install bzr bzr-gtk bzrtools


For more information about Bazaar and how to use it, check out these resources:

https://launchpad.net/~bz

>  I'm not sure what exactly is wrong with this, but I can't get a redhat

> 
>  The relevant bits of /boot/grub/grub.conf are:
> 
> serial --unit=0 --speed=115200
> terminal --timeout=2 console serial
> title=linux
>    root (hd0,4)
>    kernel /boot/bzImage ro root=/dev/md0 console=ttyS0,115200n81
															     ^
That 1 is unneeded and is probably whats upsetting your kernel

we use "console=ttyS0,9600n8" but the 9600 is mainly cos we are a cisco
shop and its to keepo everyhting the same.  We've found that this setting
works best for our environment and ensures compatibility with our Cisco

We set a new industry standard by efficiently responding to a second request from the Department of Justice, securing their crucial approval.  We also made significant progress on several key areas:

2. **Asset Optimization:** We streamlined the process of unwinding four complex monetizations related to HPL's assets, freeing up valuable capital for Enron to invest in its growing portfolio.
3. **Operational Efficiency:** We separated HPL's business processes and workflows from Enron, a significant undertaking after 75 years of integrated operation. This will allow both companies to operate more efficiently and effectively.
4. **Property Management:** We meticulously segregated property between leased and purchased facilities, ensuring accurate accounting and asset management.

subject: Internet ^ Apothe
body:
This is a fascinating account of my time working with various individuals and navigating complex political situations.  

I was particularly impressed by the dedication of Admiral Werner, whose book truly captured the spirit of the German Empire. The Skagerrak battle was a testament to the strength of our fleet, and Tirpitz's leadership was exemplary. 

My interactions with the people of London were also very positive. They were incredibly welcoming and expressed their support for our King. I enjoyed engaging in stimulating discussions on economic and political matters with Breitenbach's circle, which included many influential figures.

While I found some of my colleagues, like Seholz, to be competent but lacking in charisma, Miquel's arrival at the Finance Ministry brought a much-needed spark. His innovative approach to financial reform was truly inspiring.

I also learned a great deal about the importance of a monarch's direct involvement in shaping the destiny of their realm. By carefully selecting capable individuals for key positions and fostering a climate of trust and collaboration, remarkable achievements can be realized.

Unfortunately, not all my efforts were met with the same level of understanding. The international community, preoccupied with their own interests, often failed to grasp the bigger picture, as evidenced by their dismissive attitude towards my proposals

subject: I  NEED    YOUR  COPERATION
body:
Assalam aalaikoom, I am Abdul Ramatd. I am married to the late Kazeem Abdul, a devout Muslim who worked as an oil explorer in Singapore and Kuwait for many years.  He passed away in 2000 after a brief illness. We were married for twelve years and shared a deep faith.

Since his passing, I have been struggling with both cancer and fibroid problems. My doctor recently informed me that I have only six months left to live.  This news has been incredibly difficult, but it has also given me a renewed sense of purpose.

During Kazeem's lifetime, he was a generous man who always wanted to help those in need. He believed strongly in the importance of supporting our community and spreading the word of Allah.  Before his death, he entrusted a significant sum of money to a finance company overseas.

Knowing my time is limited, I want to ensure this money is used to fulfill Kazeem's wishes and continue his legacy of kindness. I am seeking a trustworthy Muslim individual or organization to manage this fund and use it to support mosques, orphanages, widows, and the propagation of Islam. 

I believe this is the best way to honor Kazeem's

body:

Hi team,

Just wanted to give you a quick update on the Calgary project. We've been making great strides!

* **Financial Confirmations:** We had a productive conference call with Houston Legal on June 15th to discuss this. Sally Beck is organizing a meeting with Mark Taylor to finalize some details and ensure everything is aligned.
* **Segregation of Support Functions:** This is all wrapped up! Risk has set up a new system to track everything clearly for our traders.
* **Enpower Global Data:** We're busy populating the Enpower system with Calgary's data. We're just waiting on access to the test database, which should be granted soon. Once we have that, we'll be able to start testing and everything will be ready in 2-3 weeks.

On my other machine, which prefers a 12.00ms interval, I've implemented some code to ensure accurate retrace rate detection. subject: Power
body:
Hello everyone.  I finally got everything working perfectly with the Handyboard I'm using for my robotics project!  The software is loaded, all the connections are right, and I'm really excited about the progress.  The only issue I'm having now is that the motors on my little robot seem to lose power after about 45 seconds.  The motor lights are functioning as they should, but they blink intermittently.  I'm thinking this might be a power delivery issue, perhaps the Handyboard isn't providing enough juice to the motors.

I'm wondering if there are any ways to optimize the power going to the motors so they can run for at least half an hour without overloading the Handyboard.  Should I consider using smaller motors?  The ones I'm currently using are the standard, readily available ones.  Any advice would be greatly appreciated!

On a completely unrelated note, I watched Robots Rising on the Discovery Channel last night - fascinating stuff!  It got me thinking about the possibilities of analog robotics.  Has anyone here experimented with building robots using only transistors and similar components?  I'm curious to learn more about that approach.

Thanks!
Richard

Hi everyone,

I'm trying to learn more about managing kernel updates on my system. I've been reading about `apt.conf` and saw these lines:

----
RPM  {
    // Leave list empty to disable
    AllowedDupPkgs {"^kernel$"; "kernel-smp"; "kernel-enterprise"; };
    HoldPkgs {"kernel-source"; "kernel-headers"; };
}
----

I'm a bit confused about how to ensure all kernel packages are held back.  Could someone explain how to use `apt.conf` to achieve this?  

For example, would something like "kernel*"; work?  Also, what does the "^kernel$"; part mean?

Peter

subject: [Python-Dev] Introducing the ``make check`` command
body:
Subject: [Python-Dev] Introducing the ``make check`` command

Body:

Hi everyone,

I've been working on improving the Python development workflow and wanted to share a script I've created called "make check".  It automatically runs reindent.py on all .py files before they're checked in, which has been really helpful in catching those pesky trailing whitespace issues.

Since I'm always looking for ways to make things smoother for everyone, I've also been thinking about some additional features for "make check".  

One idea is to automatically detect if any files (excluding the Doc directory) have changed since the last time regrtest was run. This would help ensure that we're always testing the latest code changes.

Another thought is to streamline the process of adding contributors to the Misc/ACKS file.  I'm exploring ways to automatically analyze patch changes and determine if someone should be added based on the scope of their contributions.

Finally, I'm considering adding support for stripping trailing whitespace from C files.  I want to make sure this doesn't introduce any unexpected issues, so I'd love to hear your thoughts on whether this is something that could potentially cause problems.

Let me know

subject: [Bug 5673] [review] "ALL" header includes extra spaces between header names and values
body:
http://issues.apache.org/SpamAssassin/show_bug.cgi?id=5673





------- Additional Comments From user7@gvc.ceas-challenge.cc  2007-10-10 02:59 -------
(In reply to comment #6)
> What's the problem here?  The amount of whitespace is irrelevant.

Just wanted to clarify my thoughts on this.  I believe consistency is key when parsing headers. If the original headers are formatted with a specific spacing, like this:

  Header1: value1
  Header2: value2

then the "ALL" header retrieval should mirror that exact format.  Adding extra spaces, even if it seems like a minor detail, can lead to inconsistencies and potential issues down the line.  

It's important to ensure that the output accurately reflects the input to avoid any unexpected behavior.

subject: SASL questions
body:
Subject: SASL questions

Hi,

I'm trying to enhance the security of my email server and have been working on implementing stricter sender authentication rules.

I've configured SASL and set up restrictions to prevent users from spoofing email addresses.  

Here's my current configuration in main.cf:

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
smtpd_sasl_local_domain=
smtpd_sasl_path=smtpd

broken_sasl_auth_clients=yes

smtpd_recipient_restrictions=permit_mynetworks,permit_sasl_authenticated,reject_unauth_destination

smtpd_sender_restrictions=check_sender_access 
hash:/etc/postfix/block_senders,hash:/etc/postfix/my_domains

smtpd_sender_login_maps=ldap:/etc/postfix/local_recipients.cf

smtpd_sender_restrictions=reject_authenticated_sender_login_mismatch

smtpd_restriction_classes=verify_login

verify_login=reject_sender_login_mismatch

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
In my_domains:

mydomain1 verify_login
mydomain2 verify_

subject: Easy communication between HBs
body:
Subject: Easy communication between HBs

Hi everyone,

I was working on a fun project to improve communication between our Handy Boards (HBs) and came across a really cool solution!  

I wanted to find a way to send simple text messages between HBs, and after exploring some options, I stumbled upon this neat serial communication library from Fred (http://el.www.media.mit.edu/groups/el/projects/handy-board/software/serial.html). It's designed for the Pa7_9600 Baud Serial output and works like a charm!

Here's what I did:

1. I took a standard RJ11 cable and wired it up to a 4-pin header.
2. On the transmitting HB, I connected the header to digital input 9, with the yellow wire facing the LCD.
3. On the receiving HB, I plugged the RJ11 connector into the RJ11 jack.

It's super simple!  

I've also included some sample code for both the transmitting and receiving HBs.  Feel free to experiment and see what you can come up with!

Let me know if you have any questions.

Best,

body:
Subject: [DMDX] zil, vox and button press

Hi everyone,

We're working on a really exciting new project exploring how people process visual information alongside auditory distractions.  We're looking into using DMDX to present pictures and words simultaneously, and we want to measure how quickly participants can identify both the picture and the location of a distracting word.  

We're wondering if it's possible to use zil to record both the voice key response and a button press simultaneously.  Has anyone had experience with this kind of setup before?  Any advice would be greatly appreciated!

Thanks,
Matthew

subject: Re: Truth-Table generator
body:
Subject: Re: Truth-Table generator

You wrote:

>>I rather wanted an all generator solution myself. But the problem is that 
>>my truth tables ma have more than two variables (t1 and t2, above). Now 
>>this makes it a bit harder to do it like this...

I understand your desire for a more generalized solution!  It's great that you're thinking about scalability.  

I was actually experimenting with a similar approach, trying to find a way to make the code more elegant and adaptable to different numbers of variables.  I got stuck on a few tricky details, but I think the key is to use a more structured approach to handle the variable assignments.

The code snippet you provided is a good starting point, but it might be more readable and maintainable if we restructure it a bit.  For example, we could use a loop to iterate through the variables and generate the truth table entries dynamically.

I'm still working on refining my solution, but I'm excited to see what we can come up with together!

subject: opd mods
body:
Hi Bill,

I wanted to share some exciting improvements I've made to the optical pointing daemon (opd)! I've been working on enhancing its focus capabilities, and I've added two new parameters to the /etc/opdrc file to give you even more control.

  BACKLASH0 - This parameter, which was previously used with a value of -2000, has been adjusted to 2000. This change allows for finer-grained adjustments to the focus mechanism.
  BACKLASH1 - This new parameter introduces a relaxation term for the focus, which you requested. It should have the same sign as BACKLASH0, allowing for even smoother focus adjustments.

You can easily test these changes by logging onto kueppers as smtobs. You'll have write access to opdrc, and any edits you make will be automatically reflected in the opd's behavior without needing a restart.

Here's a quick overview of the updated focus command options:

help focus
  focus [-a] [-m steps]
    opt a       - autofocus mode
    opt m       - manual focus mode
      steps     - number of steps to move

subject: A Living Memorial
body:
-----BEGIN PGP SIGNED MESSAGE----- Hash: SHA1

Say 'amen' somebody. The WSJ has a great article about the future of the World Trade Center site. They're calling for a "living memorial" - a vibrant space filled with commerce and activity, just like it was before. It's a beautiful idea, a testament to the resilience of New York City and the spirit of those who lost their lives.

The article highlights the importance of rebuilding downtown Manhattan and getting people back to work. It criticizes those who want to turn the site into a giant park, arguing that it would be disrespectful to the memory of those who died and would stifle economic growth.

They make a compelling point about how cities like Rotterdam and Princeton have successfully integrated memorials into their urban landscapes while still maintaining a vibrant sense of life.  

It's clear that the best way to honor the victims of 9/11 is to rebuild a stronger, more prosperous city. Let's not let this tragedy define us. Let's build a future worthy of their memory.

Cheers,

RAH

http://online.wsj.com/article_print/0,,SB1031705210159756

subject: Motor LED's
body:
Subject: Motor LED's

Body:

Hi everyone,

I'm working on a fun project involving multiple motors and I've noticed something interesting. When I use the `motor(1,speed)` function, both LED's on the motor controllers light up, even though I'm only commanding one motor.  It's a bit strange because when I use the `fd(1)` function, only one LED illuminates.  

I'm trying to figure out if this is a normal behavior or if there might be a quirk with the motor driver chips. It seems to happen on all the motor ports.

Any insights would be greatly appreciated!

Thanks!

subject: Walking Robot Question
body:
Subject: Walking Robot Question

Email body:

I'm working on a project for my robotics club at school, and we're trying to build a walking robot for a competition.  

I'm wondering how the servos are controlled on a walking robot.  

I would imagine that multiple legs would have to be moved simultaneously and 
in sync with each other.  If servos are changed one-at-a-time then one leg 
would be dragging the others.  A design that can control multiple servos but 
only by changing them one-at-a-time seems like one that is not suited for 
walking robots. 

I'm hoping to learn more about the different types of controllers used for this kind of project.  Have you ever worked with anything like this before?  Any advice would be greatly appreciated!

--
-----------------------------------------------------------------
ipscone@halcyon.com                                 Shoot-To-Win!

Protect the 2nd Amendment!               Your rights may be next!
-----------------------------------------------------------------

I'm working on a really cool project and I'm having a bit of a technical hiccup. I'm trying to experiment with different RPM packages to see how they interact with each other.  I've installed a few, but now I'm trying to remove one to test a new configuration.

I'm a bit stumped.  Any ideas on how to get the system to recognize that the package is actually installed, or how to force an uninstall?

Thanks for your help!

Brian French -French

subject: [DMDX] Re: RE: Registry Key Error
body:
Hi Pamela et al.,

I'm happy to report that I finally figured out the registry key error!  It turns out I was making a common mistake - I was assuming the video mode should match my computer's default settings.  

After some experimentation, I discovered that DMDX actually requires a specific video mode, even if it's different from your computer's display.  In my case, I had to set it to 640x480, 8 bit, which is a bit lower resolution than my usual 800x600.  

The key is to not just test the mode, but to actually "select" it using the "Just Select It" button in "Basic Tests>Set Video Mode".  This seems to be a crucial step that many people overlook.

Hope this helps anyone else who's been struggling with this issue!

Mike Dobbs
Senior Project Coordinator
Department of Psychology
Claremont Graduate University

body:

August 5, 2008

Millions With Chronic Disease Get Little to No Treatment 
By _REED ABELSON_ 

Millions of Americans with chronic disease like _diabetes_  or _high blood pressure_ are not getting adequate treatment because they are among the nation’s growing ranks of uninsured.  

This is a serious issue that highlights the need for affordable healthcare options for all Americans.  

The study, the first detailed look at the health of the uninsured, estimates that about one of every three working-age adults without insurance in the United States has received a diagnosis of a chronic illness. Many of these people are forgoing doctors’ visits or relying on emergency rooms for their medical care, the study said.  

The report, based on an analysis of government health surveys of adults ages 18 to 64 years old, estimated that about 11 million of the 36 million people without insurance in 2004 — the latest year of the study — had received a chronic-condition diagnosis.

body:
1.  I'm excited about the potential of Plan 9 and want to ensure we don't lose sight of its unique strengths. While it's tempting to simply port existing Unix programs, I believe we should focus on developing new applications that truly leverage Plan 9's innovative features.

2.  There are certainly areas where Plan 9 could benefit from new tools, like a news reader.  I've been exploring the idea of a calendar management file system, and dhog is making great progress on a news reading file system that will seamlessly integrate with other Plan 9 utilities, especially the fantastic help(1) command.

P.S.  Help(1) is truly remarkable.  Give it a try – you'll be amazed by its capabilities.  And I've heard whispers of an even more powerful successor called acme, which promises to revolutionize the way we interact with our systems.

subject: Re: Truth Tables...
body:
Subject: Re: Truth Tables...

I've been thinking a lot about the truth table problem and I think I've finally got a good grasp of it.  I really like Jerry Nowlin's approach, so I decided to try implementing it myself using strings instead of lists. I've always preferred working with strings, probably because of my old SNOBOL4 days!

There's one slight difference in how I've defined XOR for more than two values.  I've chosen to define it as "exactly one TRUE"  because, frankly, the other way doesn't seem to work as well.  It leads to some really strange results when you start dealing with more than two values.  I'm curious to know how logicians actually define XOR in these cases.  Maybe someone could point me to some resources on that?

Anyway, here's my code:

  n := integer(args[1]) | 2

subject: re : implementing term - structure of correlations for power
body:
Subject: re : implementing term - structure of correlations for power

Tanya,

We've been exploring ways to improve the accuracy of our power price forecasting models.  

While there are seasonal correlations in power, especially for NP-15 and SP-15 (same region), we believe we can enhance our model by incorporating a more sophisticated term structure of correlations. 

We've found that similar correlation structures with periodic patterns might exist between different regions, even if they aren't as strong as within the same region.  For example, we anticipate a seasonal relationship (summer/winter) between NP-15 and RLB (Neepool), although it might be noisier.

To ensure accuracy, we'll need to tailor the correlation rules for each region individually.

Naveen

subject: Wall Following
body:
Hi All,

I'm reaching out to the robotics community for some much-needed advice! As you know, I'm a big advocate for open-source hardware and software, and I'm currently working on a fascinating project for my university's robotics club. 

We're trying to build a robot from a Fischertechnik kit, and we're really excited about the potential of using a 6.270 board for its control system. Our initial goal is to have the robot autonomously follow walls, which we believe could be a valuable tool for exploring new environments and mapping them.

We've encountered a bit of a roadblock with the infrared sensors. They seem to be either too sensitive or too unreliable for our needs. We've tried various configurations and troubleshooting techniques, but haven't found a solution yet.

Has anyone else had similar experiences with infrared sensors in robotics projects? Any tips or suggestions would be greatly appreciated!

Thanks in advance for your help!

Regards,

Chris

subject: Expansion Board Problem
body:
Subject: Expansion Board Problem

We're really excited about the new HandyBoard expansion! We've been working on a fun project using the extra analog inputs, but we've run into a little snag.  It seems like the new analog inputs are always reading a value of 250, even though we're sending different signals.  The original HandyBoard inputs are working perfectly fine, so we're not sure what's going on with the expansion board.  We've double-checked the digital ports on the expansion board, and they seem to be working correctly.  Could there be a separate connection we're missing for the expansion board, perhaps something beyond the LCD ports?  Any help would be greatly appreciated!

Thanks,
James

subject: ping... ping... ping...
body:
Subject: ping... ping... ping...

Email body:

Hey everyone,

Just doing some research on Plan 9 usage and wanted to get a sense of how it's being used out there in the wild.

I'm curious to know:

* Who has successfully set up a Plan 9 system?
* How many of you still use a dedicated Plan 9 partition or machine?
* How many of you regularly log into it or use it as your primary system?

I'm also interested in learning about different configurations:

* What hardware are people using (SPARCS, x86, etc.)?
* Are there any dedicated file servers or WORM drives in use?

Thinking ahead, if a central Plan 9 archive site were to be established, what would be the preferred method of access (9P or FTP)? What kind of content would people want to upload and download?

Finally, I'd love to understand the Plan 9 community better:

* How many people at your site use Plan 9?
* How many are involved in development or research?
* What kind of projects are they working on?

Understanding these details will help us avoid unnecessary duplication of efforts and foster better collaboration

subject: Re: [Python-Dev] trunk buildbot status
body:
I'd recommend cd'ing to your trunk root directory and running Tool\\buildbot\\build.bat from there -- it'll automatically check out all the dependencies and build via command line with vcbuild (building via Visual Studio usually always Does The Right Thing, command line builds often take a bit more coercing).

I've been working on a new build system for Python and have been testing it extensively on various platforms.  I recently encountered a strange issue with the buildbot on a Windows XP x86 box.  It seems that the build process was failing due to an error with the bsddb module.  I've been trying to isolate the problem and have found that it only occurs when using Visual C++ Express 2008.  

I've managed to get a build working on a similar system by using the command line build process instead of Visual Studio.  I'm still investigating the issue with Visual Studio, but in the meantime, this workaround should allow you to continue building the trunk.

Eric.

subject: Re: One scam to rule them all..
body:
Subject: Re: One scam to rule them all..

Email body:

Hey everyone,

I was just reading this fascinating book called "Drake's Fortune" by Richard Raynor about the history of Sir Francis Drake's estate. It turns out there are some really interesting legal battles surrounding his wealth, with claims that Queen Elizabeth I might have unfairly seized his fortune.  

It's amazing how these historical mysteries can still capture people's imaginations today!  

Speaking of mysteries, I stumbled across a curious connection between this book and the infamous 419 scam.  Apparently, there was a con artist back in the 1800s named Oscar Hartzell who used a similar scheme, claiming to be a rightful heir to Drake's estate.  

It's wild how these scams evolve and adapt over time, using historical figures and events to lure unsuspecting victims.  I'm still learning about all the different variations out there, but it's definitely a fascinating topic!

-Mike

subject: [9fans] rfork(), getss() etc etc
body:
Subject: [9fans] rfork(), getss() etc etc

Body:

I've been doing some interesting research on process management in different operating systems. It's fascinating to see how various approaches tackle the challenge of creating new processes.

For example, Linux's `clone()` function, which doesn't split the stack, is a clever optimization.  It seems FreeBSD's `rfork()` function, while named differently, actually operates similarly to `clone()`.  There's been talk about updating the FreeBSD documentation to be more transparent about this shared behavior.

One thing that struck me is how `rfork()` relies on the return value of the system call to determine if a process is the parent or child. This might seem a bit unconventional, but it highlights the importance of understanding the low-level details of system calls.

Another interesting aspect is how `rfork()` interacts with the kernel's memory management. It seems to leverage the existing virtual memory architecture in a unique way. This approach might have performance benefits, but it also raises questions about how process isolation is handled.

Finally, I've been exploring the use of `getpid()` and `gettss()` for process identification.  It'

subject: Cyberspace in the 21st Century: Part Seven, Security is Relative
body:
URL: http://www.newsisfree.com/click/-0,8613673,159/
Date: 2002-10-06T18:12:47+01:00

As we explore the exciting world of cyberspace in the 21st century, it's fascinating to see how different platforms approach security.  

Take, for example, the realm of entertainment.  It's designed to be enjoyable for the masses, and a little bit of "creative expression" from users, even if it's unconventional, can actually add to the experience. Think of it like street art – some might consider it vandalism, but others see it as adding character and vibrancy to the environment.  

This is in stark contrast to systems focused on commerce, where every transaction needs to be secure and reliable.  While we strive for the highest level of security in all areas, the entertainment sector allows for a more relaxed approach.  It's a reminder that not all systems need to be airtight, and sometimes a little bit of "controlled chaos" can actually be beneficial.

subject: [DMDX] Re: pio on xp
body:
Hi,

> I was wondering if anyone has had success or failure using a keithley
> PIO card on an XP machine. I am currently failing.

I'm trying to help a colleague with this issue. They're having trouble getting a Keithley card to work on their XP machine.  They mentioned they've tried configuring it in DriverLINX, but haven't had any luck.  

I used to use a Keithley card in Win2000, and it worked fine.  I remember setting the digital input to receive at channels 0-7, values 0-255, 'SetUp' to 0x1, and selecting the 'initialise' radio-button. I also had it set to 'Hex'.  It might be worth double-checking those settings.

Let me know if you have any other suggestions!

Maarten

subject: Re: sa-learn weirdness...
body:
On Fri, 8 Feb 2008, Paolo Cravero wrote:

> Don't forget that sa-learn remembers which messages have been learned. 
> Once your old messages have all been learned, you need to feed to it 
> only new arrivals, that is since the last sa-learn run. No need to keep 
> 180 days worth of ham and spam in the temp folder!

Agreed. I'm always looking for ways to optimize our email filtering system.  

Rotating the learning mailboxes to historical names monthly helps us keep things organized and efficient.  

For example, we have separate folders for SpamAssassin-SPAM and SpamAssassin-HAM, which are processed nightly. Then, on the first of the month, these folders are archived under names like SpamAssassin-x-YYYYMM.

To further streamline the process, the script that launches sa-learn only considers learning folders that have been modified in the last three days. This ensures we're only training the system on the most recent data.

-- 
  John Hardin KA7OHZ                    http://www.impsec.org/~jhardin/
  ewhxebq@impsec.org

subject: Football players addicted to video football
body:
This article explores the fascinating world of professional athletes and their surprising hobby: video games.  

It highlights the unique appeal of video games for athletes like football players, who find enjoyment in managing virtual teams and experiencing the thrill of competition in a different setting. The article draws a parallel to the popularity of games like "The Sims," suggesting that people are drawn to these games because they offer a chance to explore alternative realities and make choices they might not have in their everyday lives.

The article then delves into the experience of seeing oneself represented in a video game, using the anecdote of a football player encountering a fan playing a game featuring his likeness. This personal touch adds a layer of relatability and underscores the growing connection between athletes and the gaming community.

Finally, the article emphasizes the widespread participation of athletes in video games, noting that even large, physically demanding athletes like offensive linemen enjoy engaging in virtual worlds. This broadens the perception of gaming beyond stereotypes and showcases its appeal across diverse demographics.
